    toc: no
    number_sections: true
  bookdown::html_document2:
    number_sections: true

csl: apa.csl

  - \usepackage{amsmath, amssymb}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{float}
  - \usepackage{placeins}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
  - \usepackage{xcolor}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup[table]{position=below,skip=6pt}

  - \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta,positioning,shadows,calc}

\begin{titlepage}
\begin{center}
	\includegraphics[width=7cm]{./logo.jpg}
\vspace{0.5in}

	\LARGE{MSc Thesis for Health Data Science}

	\Huge{A Bayesian time series Framework for Comparing On Trolley Counts Across Irish HSE Health Regions}

	\Large{Lucas Henry, Evan Bergin}

	\Large{Supervisors: Dr. Carl Scarrott, Dr. John Ferguson}

	\Large{June 2026}

\end{center}
\end{titlepage}

\clearpage
\newpage

\underline{Declaration}

We hereby certify that this material, which we now submit for assessment on the programme of study leading to the award of Master of Science is entirely our own work and had not been taken from the work of others or Artificial Intelligence, save and to the extent that such work has been cited and acknowledged within the text of our work.

ID: 25252089, 24709799

Date: 2026/06/24

\newpage

\tableofcontents

\newpage

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
fig_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "thesis-fig/")
dir.create(fig_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.pos = "!ht", out.width = "95%", fig.align = "center",
                      fig.path = fig_dir)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(readr)
library(readxl)
library(forecast)
library(scales)
library(patchwork)

# theme_minimal with all background grid lines removed
theme_thesis <- function(base_size = 11) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) + theme(panel.grid = element_blank())
}

# Selected model output directories (per 10,000 population)
selected_model <- "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k/v4.6"   # AR(2), selected model
ar1_model  <- "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k/v4.5"   # AR(1), comparison only

weekly <- read_csv("../data/raw data/weekly_by_region_202604082051.csv",
                   show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(week_start = as.Date(week_start))

region_palette <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and North East" = "#1f77b4",
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands"   = "#ff7f0e",
  "HSE Dublin and South East" = "#2ca02c",
  "HSE West and North West"   = "#d62728",
  "HSE South West"            = "#9467bd",
  "HSE Mid West"              = "#8c564b"
)

# JAGS indexes alpha[i] etc. by the row order of the model input CSV
# (regions are rows, dates are columns). Derive that ordering once so
# downstream chunks can map index -> region.
region_by_idx <- read_csv(
  "../data/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k.csv",
  col_select = 1, show_col_types = FALSE
)[[1]]
idx_by_region <- setNames(seq_along(region_by_idx), region_by_idx)
```

# Introduction \label{chap:intro}

Hospital overcrowding has become a persistent challenge for healthcare systems worldwide [@Morley2018]. When hospitals operate at or beyond capacity, the consequences extend beyond operational strain. Overcrowding in emergency departments is associated with increased patient mortality, delayed time-critical treatments, longer waiting times, and reduced quality of care [@Asplin2003]. Understanding and monitoring hospital load is therefore not only a resource management concern but a patient safety issue.

In Ireland, hospital congestion is commonly monitored using the number of patients recorded on hospital trolleys. These patients have been admitted through emergency departments but remain without an available inpatient bed, often receiving care in temporary spaces such as corridors or emergency wards. Trolley counts are widely used as a daily indicator of hospital system load and are frequently referenced in public reporting and policy discussions concerning hospital capacity and healthcare performance [@HSE2026].

The Health Service Executive publishes daily trolley counts across hospitals nationwide. Despite their widespread use, these counts are typically presented as raw daily totals, offering limited insight into the underlying dynamics of hospital demand. Raw counts do not adjust for population size, meaning regions with larger populations will naturally record higher totals regardless of the relative load on their systems. They also do not account for temporal patterns such as seasonal fluctuations in illness demand, nor do they allow systematic or probabilistic comparisons between healthcare regions.

Ireland's six HSE health regions differ substantially in population size, age structure, and acute hospital capacity. The three eastern Dublin regions, Dublin and North East (1.19 million), Dublin and Midlands (1.08 million), and Dublin and South East (0.97 million), are the most populous and carry the largest concentrations of inpatient beds, with Dublin and North East alone accounting for 2,814 available beds [@CSO_HHR01; @hse_beds_pq_2025]. The three western regions, West and North West (760,000), South West (741,000), and Mid West (413,000), serve smaller and more dispersed populations across predominantly rural catchments, with acute services concentrated in fewer centres [@CSO_HHR01]. These structural differences are compounded by age: people aged 65 and over account for approximately 15% of the national population but 40% of hospital discharges, and the share of older residents varies across regions, with the western regions carrying a proportionally higher burden [@CSO_FY006B; @CSO_HHR02; @brick_projections_2025]. Taken together, these demographic and capacity differences mean that raw hospital activity counts are poorly suited to cross-regional comparison, as a region with a larger population will naturally record higher absolute counts regardless of the relative pressure on its system, making per capita standardisation essential for any meaningful assessment of regional hospital load. Figure \@ref(fig:eda-region-pop) summarises these contrasts across the six regions: population, available inpatient beds, budget, and population aged 65 and over.

```{r eda-region-pop, fig.cap="HSE health region characteristics: (a) 2022 health region population, (b) 2025 available inpatient beds, (c) 2026 region budgets, (d) 2022 count of population aged 65+ (thousands). Regions are ordered by population (largest first) and shared across panels so each region occupies the same column.", fig.width=11, fig.height=6, dev="cairo_pdf"}
.add_hse <- function(x) paste("HSE", x)

pop_df <- read_csv("../supplementary data/2022_hr_population.csv",
                   show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  rename(Region = `HSE Health Regions`, Population = `Number of persons`) |>
  distinct(Region, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  mutate(Region = .add_hse(Region), Population_k = Population / 1000) |>
  arrange(desc(Population))

pop_order <- pop_df$Region

beds_df <- read_csv("../supplementary data/2025_hr_beds_per_hospital.csv",
                    show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  group_by(region) |>
  summarise(total_inpatient  = sum(tx_inpatient,       na.rm = TRUE),
            closed_inpatient = sum(closures_inpatient, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(Region = .add_hse(region),
         available_inpatient = total_inpatient - closed_inpatient) |>
  select(Region, available_inpatient)

budget_df <- read_excel("../supplementary data/2026_hr_budget.xlsx") |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Region", values_to = "Budget") |>
  mutate(Budget_m = Budget / 1000)   # source is in EUR thousands → millions

age_df <- read_csv("../supplementary data/2022_hr_age_dist.csv",
                   show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  filter(`Age Group` != "All ages") |>
  group_by(`HSE Regions`, `Age Group`) |>
  summarise(VALUE = mean(VALUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(`Age Group` %in% c("65 - 69 years", "70 - 74 years",
                            "75 - 79 years", "80 - 84 years",
                            "85 years and over")) |>
  group_by(`HSE Regions`) |>
  summarise(Pct_65plus = sum(VALUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(Region = .add_hse(`HSE Regions`)) |>
  select(Region, Pct_65plus)

combined <- tibble(Region = pop_order) |>
  left_join(pop_df    |> select(Region, Population_k), by = "Region") |>
  left_join(beds_df,                                   by = "Region") |>
  left_join(budget_df |> select(Region, Budget_m),     by = "Region") |>
  left_join(age_df,                                    by = "Region") |>
  mutate(Pop_65plus_k = Population_k * Pct_65plus / 100,
         Region = factor(Region, levels = pop_order))

.bar <- function(df, col, ylab, title, fmt) {
  ggplot(df, aes(Region, .data[[col]], fill = Region)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.6) +
    geom_text(aes(label = fmt(.data[[col]])),
              vjust = -0.5, size = 2.6) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = region_palette, guide = "none") +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = ylab, title = title) +
    theme_thesis(base_size =10) +
    theme(axis.text.x  = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 7),
          plot.title   = element_text(size = 10))
}

p1 <- .bar(combined, "Population_k",
           "Population (thousands)",     "(a) 2022 health region population",
           \(v) sprintf("%.0fk", v))
p2 <- .bar(combined, "available_inpatient",
           "Available inpatient beds",   "(b) 2025 available inpatient beds",
           \(v) format(v, big.mark = ","))
p3 <- .bar(combined, "Budget_m",
           "Budget (€ millions)",   "(c) 2026 region budgets",
           \(v) sprintf("€%.0fm", v))
p4 <- .bar(combined, "Pop_65plus_k",
           "Population aged 65+ (thousands)", "(d) 2022 population aged 65+ (count)",
           \(v) sprintf("%.0fk", v))

(p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4)
```

Hospital demand is influenced by multiple factors including population demographics, seasonal illness patterns, healthcare infrastructure, and patterns of emergency department admissions. These factors generate complex temporal dynamics in hospital congestion that cannot be fully understood through descriptive statistics alone. Policy responses such as capacity management protocols, discharge planning initiatives, and operational resets further shape observed demand patterns, making it important to account for known interventions when modelling trolley counts over time. Hospital demand and the congestion it generates are inherently temporal: counts recorded in one week are likely to be influenced by counts in preceding weeks, congestion tends to persist until patients are discharged or capacity is increased, and seasonal patterns in respiratory illness mean that load is not uniform across the year. time series modelling provides a useful framework for analysing such data, as it allows researchers to account for these sources of temporal dependence and recurring structure, enabling the underlying level of each region to be estimated more reliably and systematic patterns to be separated from week-to-week noise [@Shumway2017].

Bayesian statistical methods provide a natural framework for incorporating uncertainty into such analyses. Rather than producing single point estimates, Bayesian models treat parameters as probability distributions and update these using observed data, producing probabilistic estimates that are often more informative than traditional approaches [@Gelman2013]. The Bayesian ranking approach of @Laird1989 extends this to produce probabilistic orderings of units that account for uncertainty in the underlying parameter estimates, making it particularly well suited to regional comparison in healthcare settings.

The HSE organises its hospital network into six health regions: Dublin and Midlands, Dublin and North East, Dublin and South East, Mid West, South West, and West and North West. Daily trolley counts at hospital level have been publicly available since January 2023 [@HSE2026], providing a consistent basis for regional comparison over time. This study develops a Bayesian time series framework for comparing hospital trolley load across these six regions, using weekly trolley counts from January 2023 to April 2026 sourced from the HSE Urgent and Emergency Care daily report. Trolley counts are standardised by regional population and expressed as rates per 10,000 residents to allow meaningful cross-regional comparison. The modelling framework decomposes regional trolley rates into a regional level, an annual cycle, New Year effects, and where applicable, the impact of known operational interventions, with an autoregressive component capturing week-to-week persistence. A notable feature of the data is the HSE Mid West operational reset in August 2024, during which scheduled care was deferred to address acute overcrowding, and the modelling framework is designed to isolate the effect of such events from the underlying annual cycle and level.

The primary objective of this research is to provide a statistically rigorous basis for comparing hospital load across Irish HSE regions, identify the temporal and operational features driving trolley counts, and demonstrate that Bayesian time series modelling of routinely collected administrative data can yield policy relevant insights to support capacity planning and resource allocation decisions across the Irish health system.

# Literature Review \label{chap:litreview}

Hospital overcrowding and its consequences have been extensively documented in the international literature. Overcrowding in emergency departments is associated with increased patient waiting times, reduced quality of care, and greater strain on healthcare staff [@Asplin2003; @Morley2018]. In Ireland, this issue has been persistent and politically prominent, with trolley counts serving as the primary public metric of hospital system pressure for over two decades. At a descriptive level, persistent high trolley counts in western hospitals, particularly University Hospital Limerick and University Hospital Galway, have been documented repeatedly in INMO Trolley Watch reports, with both hospitals consistently ranking among the most overcrowded in the country [@inmo_trolleywatch_2024; @bowers_risk_2026]. However, these raw counts have not previously been subjected to a formal regional comparison that controls for population size, seasonal dynamics, and known operational events.

A well-established driver of emergency demand in Ireland is seasonal respiratory illness. @Mulroe2019 analysed adult emergency hospitalisations nationally from 2015 to 2018 and found that while overall emergency admissions were only marginally higher in winter, respiratory conditions increased by 40.5% relative to summer, driven primarily by chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, pneumonia, and lower respiratory tract infection. This winter surge is consistent with patterns documented internationally and provides direct epidemiological motivation for modelling seasonal structure explicitly rather than treating it as noise.

Internationally, Bayesian and time series methods have been applied to hospital demand data across a range of settings, though the predominant focus has been short-term operational forecasting at individual hospital sites rather than sustained regional comparison. @Sahu2014 develop a hierarchical Bayesian model for short-term prediction of hospital admissions in Cardiff and Southampton, incorporating seasonal and meteorological variables and propagating forecast uncertainty through the posterior distribution; this work is methodologically relevant to the present study but is oriented towards prediction rather than characterisation of long-run regional differences. @Upshur2005 apply time series methods to population-based hospital admission data across the province of Ontario from 1988 to 2001, finding systematic seasonal patterns across 33 of the 52 most common admission diagnoses; their work establishes that hospital admissions have predictable seasonal structure that can be modelled formally, though it does not extend to probabilistic regional comparison or Bayesian inference. More recently, @Mellor2023 use hierarchical generalised additive models to forecast influenza hospital admissions across English sub-regions, demonstrating that hierarchical structures can exploit shared information across geographic units to improve forecasts. While these studies collectively establish the methodological foundations on which the present study draws, none combines Bayesian time series decomposition with population-standardised rates and probabilistic regional ranking applied to routinely collected administrative data over a sustained multi-year period. The closest parallel in spirit is the work of @Jacob2024, who find substantial residual variation of the order of three to one in per-person emergency department attendance rates across local areas in England after controlling for population risk factors, demonstrating that raw counts systematically obscure underlying regional disparities, a finding that directly motivates the population-standardised approach adopted here.

A common approach for capturing annual cycles in hospital data is trigonometric regression, in which cosine and sine terms at the relevant frequency are included as fixed covariates within a regression or state-space framework, allowing both the amplitude and phase of the cycle to be estimated from the data [@Shumway2017]. This specification has practical advantages over dummy-variable approaches: it uses two parameters to represent a full annual cycle regardless of the length of the series, and the amplitude and peak timing are directly interpretable from the estimated coefficients. The present study adopts this approach within a Bayesian framework.

Bayesian methods have an established tradition in hospital performance profiling and regional comparison. @Laird1989 introduced an empirical Bayes ranking approach that treats unit-level parameters as draws from a common prior, producing probabilistic rank distributions that account for estimation uncertainty. This approach has subsequently been applied to hospital profiling, where ranking institutions on a single metric risks overstating certainty in orderings that may be largely driven by chance variation [@Austin2002]. @MacDermott2025 demonstrate the practical value of the Laird and Louis framework in a comparative performance setting, applying Bayesian ranking to Olympic medal table data and showing that credible intervals on rank positions frequently span several places, illustrating that apparent differences in performance may not be statistically credible. The present study applies this framework to regional hospital trolley rates, where the question of whether observed differences in burden reflect genuine structural disparities or sampling noise is of direct policy relevance.

Within the Irish context, the most directly relevant study is @Lynch2019, who apply a structural breaks time series analysis to weekly trolley counts across four HSE regions from 2005 to 2015, finding significant post-reconfiguration changes in trolley numbers in three of the four regions studied. This is the only published time series analysis of Irish trolley data at a regional level that we are aware of. However, the data used are sourced from INMO Trolley Watch reports, which count differently from the HSE report used here by including surge-bed and ward overflow patients the HSE excludes [@rte_trolley_2023]. They further exclude weekends and bank holidays, cover only four regions under the pre-2023 HSE structure, and are not population standardised, limiting the comparability of pressure levels across regions. The frequentist interrupted time series framework is furthermore designed to evaluate the effect of a specific policy intervention rather than to characterise underlying temporal dynamics or support systematic regional comparison with quantified uncertainty.

These gaps motivate the present study. No published analysis has applied a Bayesian time series framework to characterise and compare long-run hospital pressure across Irish health regions with explicit uncertainty quantification, nor has any study examined trolley data under the current six-region HSE structure established in 2023. The present work addresses both gaps by developing a Bayesian time series model that estimates region-specific pressure levels, accounts for seasonal and calendar dynamics, and enables principled probabilistic comparison of regions on a population-standardised basis.

# Data \label{chap:data}
This section outlines the data used in the analysis: collecting the raw data, preparing it for modelling, and exploring the series to identify important features.

## Data Sources \label{sect:data-source}
Daily on trolley counts are scraped from the publicly available HSE Urgent and Emergency Care daily report [@HSE2026]. The report is published as an HTML page with no known API. It has been published daily since 1st of January 2023 and remains actively updated. For our paper, we collected data from the 1st of January 2023 to the 5th of April 2026. The HSE Urgent and Emergency Care daily report contains counts of patients on ED trolleys, ward trolleys, surge capacity in use, delayed transfers of care, the total number waiting over 24 hours, and the number of those over 75 waiting over 24 hours. Our analysis uses the total on trolley counts, a sum of ED and ward trolleys. Total counts are chosen because they are the most direct measure of the burden of patients on trolleys in each region, and they are a widely reported measure of hospital crowding [@HSE2026]. All code for scraping, preprocessing, model fitting, and figure generation is available at https://github.com/hotsoupisgood/hse-urgent-care-report-trolley-analysis, supporting full reproduction of the results.

## Scraping \label{sect:data-pipeline}
Scraping is performed using Python, iterating through each day's report from the 1st of January 2023 to the 5th of April 2026. The report is scraped from the URL: `https://uec.hse.ie/uec/TGAR.php?EDDATE=DD%2FMM%2FYYYY`, where `EDDATE` is the date in `DD/MM/YYYY` (day/month/year) format, with `%2F` being encoded slashes. On this report page, data is displayed in the `<table>` tag, which produces a structured HTML table, where rows are coded inside `<tr>` tags, and cells are coded inside `<td>` tags. The scraper extracts the date, region, and total daily on trolley count from the table, and saves it to a CSV file for further processing covered in the following section.

## Preprocessing \label{sect:data-preprocessing}
Preprocessing involves validating, cleaning, and transforming the raw daily hospital counts into the weekly regional counts. The daily counts are summed into weekly counts, with Monday designated as the first day of the week. Hospital counts are summed into regional weekly totals and a final CSV is produced which is explored throughout the rest of the analysis. 
  
\newpage

## Exploratory Data Analysis \label{sect:data-EDA}
The first step in exploring the preprocessed weekly trolley counts is to identify key patterns in the time series. The second step is to gather further evidence for the patterns with more rigorous plots (Figures \@ref(fig:eda-newyear), \@ref(fig:eda-midwest), \@ref(fig:eda-psd), \@ref(fig:eda-pacf)). In Figure \@ref(fig:eda-weekly-overview) each region is a different coloured line. The horizontal axis is time from initial release of the report on the 1st of January 2023 to the 5th of April 2026. The Figure suggests three key features: an annual cycle peaking near winter; a sharp dip each New Year, marked by light blue bands; and an isolated dip in HSE Mid West during August 2024, marked by the light red band (the 8th to 18th of August window). Each feature is examined with supporting evidence and sources in the following paragraphs.


```{r eda-weekly-overview, fig.cap="Weekly total on trolley counts by HSE region from 2023 to 2026. Light blue bands mark the New Year weeks across all regions; the light red band marks the Mid West reset window (8th to 18th of August 2024).", fig.width=9, fig.height=3}
# set 1 week before and after ny 
ny_anchors <- as.Date(c("2023-01-01", "2024-01-01", "2025-01-01", "2026-01-01"))
ny_bands <- data.frame(xmin = ny_anchors - 7, xmax = ny_anchors + 7)
# mw effect band
mw_band <- data.frame(xmin = as.Date("2024-08-08"), xmax = as.Date("2024-08-18"))

ggplot(weekly, aes(week_start, total_trolleys, color = region)) +
  geom_rect(data = ny_bands, inherit.aes = FALSE,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_rect(data = mw_band, inherit.aes = FALSE,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            fill = "lightcoral", alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = region_palette) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Weekly on trolley counts", color = "Region") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

### New Year Dip \label{sect:data-EDA-nydip}

An annual dip around the New Year aligns with the HSE practice of discharging patients over Christmas to prepare for a January surge driven by increased respiratory illness [@department_of_health_minister_2023] and delayed hospital visits [@noauthor_hse_2026]. Figure \@ref(fig:eda-newyear) shows the New Year dip in HSE Dublin and Midlands (an example region) for 2024, 2025, and 2026 as traces. The dashed line at week 0 marks the 1st of January, and each week is positioned by its starting date as a point on the graph. A consequence of weekly aggregation is the 1st of January falls on a different weekday each year. So the New Year week rate is split unevenly across adjacent weekly bins, which can lead to misinterpretation of the data. The plot shows that the New Year reaches a minimal level approximately 1 to 2 weeks before the New Year week. Notably, 2026 has the lowest dip in the series; the remaining regions follow the same pattern, except HSE Dublin and South East where 2026 has the highest dip across available years. 

```{r eda-newyear, fig.cap="New Year for HSE Dublin and Midlands, weekly total trolleys in the 8 weeks either side of the 1st of January for each year in the series.", fig.width=8, fig.height=3}
# 2024 onward only: the 2023 New Year has no pre-Christmas 2022 data for the 8-week-before window
ny_anchors <- as.Date(c("2024-01-01", "2025-01-01", "2026-01-01"))

ny_close <- weekly |>
  filter(region == "HSE Dublin and Midlands") |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(closest_ny = ny_anchors[which.min(abs(as.numeric(week_start - ny_anchors)))],
         weeks_from_ny = as.numeric(week_start - closest_ny) / 7,
         year = format(closest_ny, "%Y")) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(abs(weeks_from_ny) <= 8)

ggplot(ny_close, aes(weeks_from_ny, total_trolleys, color = year)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 1.6) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.4) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-8, 8, 2)) +
  labs(x = "Weeks relative to New Year", y = "Weekly on trolley counts", color = "Year") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11)
```

### Mid West Reset \label{sect:data-EDA-MWReset}

In contrast to the New Year dip, the Mid West dip is not observed in other regions, and does not align with any known cycle. The red shaded period in Figure \@ref(fig:eda-midwest) shows the HSE Mid West region during what has been reported as the reset period. In response to high demand, HSE Mid West delayed scheduled care to focus on unscheduled care from the 8th to 18th of August 2024 [@michelle_norris_et_al_advice_2025]. The reset period overlaps with the dip in on trolley counts in the Mid West region as seen in Figure \@ref(fig:eda-midwest). This dip is not observed in the other regions, seen by comparing the non-Mid West grey lines in Figure \@ref(fig:eda-midwest) with the Mid West red line, suggesting the dip is from the reset rather than an effect common to all regions. The New Year dip and Mid West reset both lack obvious parametric forms, and will require a block effect model which will be discussed in Section \@ref(sect:model-development). In the following sections we will examine the spectral and autocorrelation properties of the series to determine how to capture these parametric effects.

```{r eda-midwest, fig.cap="HSE Mid West weekly trolley totals from May to November 2024. The shaded period marks the 8th to 18th of August Mid West reset period. The grey lines are non-Mid West regions over the same time span.", fig.width=8, fig.height=3}
# mw_band (reset window) is defined in the eda-weekly-overview chunk above
mw_window <- weekly |>
  filter(week_start >= as.Date("2024-05-01"),
         week_start <= as.Date("2024-11-01"))

mw_df <- mw_window |> filter(region == "HSE Mid West")
mw_others <- mw_window |> filter(region != "HSE Mid West")

ggplot(mw_df, aes(week_start, total_trolleys)) +
  geom_rect(data = mw_band, inherit.aes = FALSE,
            aes(xmin = xmin, xmax = xmax, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
            fill = "lightcoral", alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_line(data = mw_others, aes(group = region), color = "grey50",
            linewidth = 0.4, alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_point(data = mw_others, aes(group = region), color = "grey50",
             size = 1.2, alpha = 0.35) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Weekly on trolley counts", color = "Year") +
  geom_line(color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_point(color = "firebrick", size = 1.6) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 month", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11)
```

### Spectral Analysis \label{sect:data-EDA-PSD}

The spectral analysis is performed using a power spectral density (PSD), estimated by the periodogram below. The Fourier transform represents a signal as a sum of sinusoids at particular frequencies, each with an amplitude and phase [@Smith1999]. The PSD reports the power, the squared amplitude, at each frequency and discards phase. The transform is computed via the fast Fourier transform (FFT). On the vertical axis, high power indicates a strong cycle at that frequency. The periodogram in Figure \@ref(fig:eda-psd) shows high power particularly in the 52 week period, and also heightened power near the 1-month, 2-month, and 6-month marks (4, 8, and 26 weeks).

The regions form two groups by their power at the annual cycle. HSE South West, Dublin and North East, and Dublin and Midlands group with the highest power, and at the lowest power HSE Dublin and South East, Mid West, and West and North West group together. The annual cycle is the most notable cycle in the series. But given we only have three full years of New Year data (2024, 2025, and 2026) any estimates of the annual cycle will be imprecise.

```{r eda-psd, fig.cap="Periodogram of weekly trolley counts for each HSE region. Dashed lines mark reference periods at 4, 8, 26, and 52 weeks. The horizontal axis is the period in weeks on a log scale.", fig.width=8, fig.height=3}
psd_df <- weekly |>
  group_by(region) |>
  group_modify(~ {
    s <- spec.pgram(.x$total_trolleys, plot = FALSE, spans = c(3, 3))
    data.frame(period = 1 / s$freq, power = s$spec)
  }) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(period <= 60)

ggplot(psd_df, aes(period, power, color = region)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = region_palette) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(4, 8, 26, 52), linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = c(4, 8, 26, 52), y = Inf,
           label = c("1 month (4 wk)", "2 month (8 wk)", "6 month (26 wk)", "Annual (52 wk)"),
           hjust = 1.1, vjust = 1.4, size = 3.2, color = "grey30") +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(2, 4, 8, 12, 26, 52)) +
  labs(x = "Weeks (log)", y = "Power", color = "Region") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r eda-psd-raw, fig.cap="Raw (unsmoothed) periodogram of weekly trolley counts for each HSE region. Dashed lines mark reference periods at 4, 8, 26, and 52 weeks. The horizontal axis is the period in weeks on a log scale.", fig.width=8, fig.height=3}
psd_raw_df <- weekly |>
  group_by(region) |>
  group_modify(~ {
    s <- spec.pgram(.x$total_trolleys, plot = FALSE)
    data.frame(period = 1 / s$freq, power = s$spec)
  }) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(period <= 60)

ggplot(psd_raw_df, aes(period, power, color = region)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = region_palette) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(4, 8, 26, 52), linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = c(4, 8, 26, 52), y = Inf,
           label = c("1 month (4 wk)", "2 month (8 wk)", "6 month (26 wk)", "Annual (52 wk)"),
           hjust = 1.1, vjust = 1.4, size = 3.2, color = "grey30") +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(2, 4, 8, 12, 26, 52)) +
  labs(x = "Weeks (log)", y = "Power", color = "Region") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


Beyond the annual cycle, the periodogram shows weaker peaks just off the 1-month, 2-month, and 6-month marks (4, 8, and 26 weeks). These peaks may not centre on their true period due to the limited number of data points. They could represent monthly, 2-month, or 6-month cycles. A 6-month cycle could be influenced by hospital doctor (NCHD) changeovers in January and July [@pe_global_navigating_nchd]. They could also be harmonics of the annual cycle.

The 6-month peak is clearest in HSE West and North West and Dublin and Midlands. At the 2-month mark HSE Dublin and Midlands has the highest peak. HSE West and North West, Dublin and North East, Mid West, and South West also peak in this area. The 1-month peak is the weakest. HSE West and North West and Dublin and Midlands are strongest and HSE Dublin and South East is weakest.

These peaks do not sit on the 1-month, 2-month, or 6-month marks. They fall closer to the harmonics of the annual cycle, which lie at 52/n weeks (26, 8.7, and 4.3 weeks), than to the calendar months. This favours harmonics of a non-sinusoidal annual cycle over genuine monthly cycles. The evidence is still not clear. Three years of weekly data may place the peaks imprecisely, so the periodogram ultimately cannot confirm a sub-annual cycle. No conclusion is drawn from the spectrum alone. Each candidate cycle is tested as a model component and compared by DIC (Table \@ref(tab:results-dic-table)).

### Autocorrelation \label{sect:data-EDA-ACF}
The autocorrelation function (ACF) and partial autocorrelation function (PACF) are used to analyse the temporal dependencies in the weekly trolley counts. Where the ACF is $\rho_k = \operatorname{corr}(x_t,\, x_{t-k})$ and the PACF is $\phi_{kk} = \operatorname{corr}\!\left(x_t,\, x_{t-k} \mid x_{t-1}, \ldots, x_{t-k+1}\right)$, the correlation at lag $k$ with the intermediate lags partialled out. In Figure \@ref(fig:eda-pacf) in the vertical axis is the autocorrelation, the horizontal axis is the lag in weeks, and the dashed lines mark the 95% confidence band; bars beyond it are significant. Figure \@ref(fig:eda-pacf) shows the ACF (a) and PACF (b) for HSE Dublin and Midlands. The ACF decays slowly and remains significant across many lags, consistent with an annual cycle. The PACF has a large lag 1 value that drops off sharply, indicating an AR(1) effect. The PACF also has significant peaks at 3, 4, and 5 weeks indicating an additional monthly cycle.

```{r eda-pacf, fig.cap="(a) Autocorrelation and (b) partial autocorrelation functions of weekly trolley totals for HSE Dublin and Midlands. Dashed lines mark the 95\\% confidence band.", fig.width=8, fig.height=4}
dm_ts <- weekly |>
  filter(region == "HSE Dublin and Midlands") |>
  arrange(week_start) |>
  pull(total_trolleys)

acf_p <- ggAcf(dm_ts, lag.max = 60) +
  labs(title = NULL, x = "Lag (weeks)", y = "ACF") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11)

pacf_p <- ggPacf(dm_ts, lag.max = 60) +
  labs(title = NULL, x = "Lag (weeks)", y = "PACF") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11)

(acf_p / pacf_p) + plot_annotation(tag_levels = "a")
```


# Methods \label{chap:methods}
This section covers the four steps that take the explored series to a regional ranking: scaling the response (Section \@ref(sect:standardisation)), developing the model (Section \@ref(sect:model-development)), fitting it with Bayesian inference (Section \@ref(sect:bayesian-inference)), and ranking the regions. The primary scale throughout is the rate per 10,000 population.

## Response Scaling \label{sect:standardisation}
Ranking regions by the raw on trolley counts would be misleading, as ranks would primarily reflect the population size of each region. For example, HSE Dublin and North East has the largest population and the highest raw on trolley counts, but this does not necessarily mean it is performing worse than other regions. To investigate other values contributing to our ranking, several denominators are used to scale the response. Scaling allows comparisons of the regions on an equal level, as the raw counts are influenced by factors such as population size, funding and bed capacity. The per 10,000 population scaling is the primary measure used throughout, as it most directly controls for differences in region size. Sensitivity to the alternative denominators (budget, beds, and population aged 65+) is examined in Section \@ref(sect:results-sensitivity).


### Per 10,000 population

The primary denominator used is the population of each health region in 2022. The weekly on trolley count is then divided by this population and scaled to a rate per 10,000 population:

$$
y_{j,t} = \frac{\text{weekly trolley count}_{j,t}}{\text{health region population}_j} \times 10{,}000.
$$

Per 10,000 population is the primary scaling as it is the most direct way to control for differences in demand between regions. So our results default to this scaling. The scales below largely follow similar distributions to population size.

### Per €1 billion of regional budget
To control for funding differentials, on trolley counts are scaled by the 2026 HSE regional budget allocation, expressing the outcome as trolleys per €1 billion of regional budget.

$$
y_{j,t} = \frac{\text{weekly trolley count}_{j,t}}{\text{health region budget in billion (€)}_j}.
$$

Regions with lower on trolley counts per €1 billion of regional budget may be considered more efficient.


### Per 100 inpatient beds

A similar efficiency measure is applied to inpatient beds in each region in 2025. The number of inpatient beds is used as a proxy for the space capacity of each region. Regions that have a lower on trolley count per 100 inpatient beds may be considered more efficient at handling patients within their allocated space. The outcome is expressed as trolleys per 100 inpatient beds:

$$
y_{j,t} = \frac{\text{weekly trolley count}_{j,t}}{\text{number of health region inpatient beds}_j} \times 100.
$$

### Per 1,000 population aged 65+

People aged 65+ disproportionately utilise hospitals [@brick_projections_2025]. 65+ make up `r round(subset(read.csv("../supplementary data/discharge_age_65plus_vs_under65.csv"), metric=="65plus_pop_share_pct")$value)`% of the population but `r round(subset(read.csv("../supplementary data/discharge_age_65plus_vs_under65.csv"), metric=="65plus_discharge_share_pct")$value)`% of discharges [@CSO_FY006B; @CSO_DHA74]. Scaling by region
population aged 65+ controls for differences in age structure between regions [@CSO_HHR01; @CSO_HHR02].

$$
\text{population aged 65+}_j = \text{health region population}_j \times \frac{\%~\text{aged 65+}_j}{100}.
$$

The scaling is then expressed as trolleys per 1,000 population aged 65+:

$$
y_{j,t} = \frac{\text{weekly trolley count}_{j,t}}{\text{population aged 65+}_j} \times 1{,}000.
$$

## Model Comparison \label{sect:model-comparison}

Components are compared with the deviance information criterion (DIC) [@spiegelhalter_bayesian_2002]. The DIC is used in the initial model development to support the observations from the exploratory analysis. The DIC ranks candidate models by their ability to predict new data of the same kind. It is the posterior mean deviance plus $p_D$, the effective number of parameters. The $p_D$ term penalises complexity and protects against overfitting. A smaller DIC suggests better predictive ability. A single DIC value has no meaning on its own. It is read only relative to other models on the same data. In general, a component is retained when it lowers DIC.

## Model Development \label{sect:model-development}

Using the scaled responses discussed in the previous section, the model is now specified. The model is developed iteratively with increasing complexity, as in @gelman_exploratory_2004, adding components from EDA and residual patterns. Each observation is modelled as a regional mean $\mu_{j,t}$ plus two autoregressive terms, $\mathcal{E}^{1}_{j,t}$ and $\mathcal{E}^{2}_{j,t}$. The mean captures the level and cycle structure, and the AR terms capture residual autocorrelation at lag 1 and lag 2. Initialisation of the first two lags is achieved via:

$$
y_{j,1} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{j,1},\ \tau_j^{-1}\right); \qquad
y_{j,2} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{j,2} + \mathcal{E}^{1}_{j,2},\ \tau_j^{-1}\right); \qquad
y_{j,t} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{j,t} + \mathcal{E}^{1}_{j,t} + \mathcal{E}^{2}_{j,t},\ \tau_j^{-1}\right), \quad t > 2.
$$

Here $y_{j,t}$ is the scaled trolley count for region $j$ at week $t$, $\tau_j$ is the region-specific precision, and the AR terms use coefficients $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ shared across all regions.

The mean ($\mu_{j,t}$) component is composed of: a regional level ($\alpha_j$), an annual cycle ($\mathcal{C}_{j,t}^{52}$), a regional New Year effect ($\mathcal{R}_{j,t}$), and the Mid West reset effect ($\mathcal{MW}_t$). The mean component is fitted for each region, $j$, and week, $t$: $\mu_{j,t} = \alpha_j + \mathcal{C}_{j,t}^{52} + \mathcal{R}_{j,t} + \mathbf{1}(j = \text{MW})\,\mathcal{MW}_t.$

The regional level and annual cycle are motivated by the exploratory analysis. Regions sit at different levels across the series (Figure \@ref(fig:eda-weekly-overview)), so each region is given its own level $\alpha_j$. Removing it from the model and using one global level raises DIC from `r round(read_csv(file.path("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k","v4.6","dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)` to `r round(read_csv(file.path("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k","v4.12","dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)` (Appendix Table \@ref(tab:app-regional-structure-dic)), so the regional level is kept on DIC grounds. The series peaks in winter and the periodogram has a dominant 52 week peak (Figures \@ref(fig:eda-weekly-overview), \@ref(fig:eda-psd)), so an annual cycle is added. Its amplitude and phase are allowed to vary by region. This is not chosen on DIC. A single shared cycle in fact has a slightly lower DIC of `r round(read_csv(file.path("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k","v4.13","dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)` against `r round(read_csv(file.path("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k","v4.6","dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)`, with the same deviance. The region-specific cycle is kept because the regional amplitude and phase are the quantities of interest (Section \@ref(sect:results-cycle-amplitude)). The small DIC cost buys the regional comparison at no loss of fit.

For this model, the annual cycle is a sine wave reformulated as a linear regression: 
$$\mathcal{C}_{j,t}^{52} = A_j\cos(\frac{2\pi t}{52}+\phi_j)= \beta_j \cos\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi t}{52}\right) + \gamma_j \sin\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi t}{52}\right)$$. 
Where $\beta_j = A_j\cos(\phi_j)$ and $\gamma_j = -A_j\sin(\phi_j)$. From the posterior of $\beta_j$ and $\gamma_j$, the amplitude $A_j = \sqrt{\beta_j^2 + \gamma_j^2}$ and peak week $m_j=\tan^{-1}(\gamma_j/\beta_j)$.

Because there is no obvious parametric form, the regional New Year effect is modelled as a block effect spanning the week containing the 1st of January, the two weeks before, and one week after:

\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{R}_{j,t} &= \delta_{j,t}\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{New Year week} - 2) \\
                  &+ \delta_{j,t}\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{New Year week} - 1) \\
                  &+ \delta_{j,t}\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{New Year week}) \\
                  &+ \delta_{j,t}\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{New Year week} + 1).
\end{aligned}
\label{eq:base-newyear}
\end{equation}

In the Mid West region, from the 8th to the 18th of August 2024, scheduled care was deferred to focus on unscheduled care. Weeks are referenced by their Monday start date, so the deferral falls in the week beginning Monday the 5th of August 2024. The reset period also has no obvious parametric form, so it is also modelled as a weekly block effect. It is captured with the $\mathcal{MW}_t$ component, which spans the weeks of the Mid West reset period, and three weeks after to capture a return to the regional level. The region gate $\mathbf{1}(j = \text{MW})$ in the mean restricts the effect to the Mid West, so $\mathcal{MW}_t$ is indexed by week alone:

\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{MW}_t &= \kappa_t\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{Aug 5 2024 week}) \\
               &+ \kappa_t\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{Aug 12 2024 week}) \\
               &+ \kappa_t\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{Aug 19 2024 week}) \\
               &+ \kappa_t\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{Aug 26 2024 week}) \\
               &+ \kappa_t\,\mathbf{1}(t = \text{Sep 2 2024 week}).
\end{aligned}
\label{eq:base-mwreset}
\end{equation}

Week-to-week residual autocorrelation is modelled with two autoregressive terms: $\mathcal{E}^{1}_{j,t} = \phi_1\,(y_{j,t-1} - \mu_{j,t-1})$ at lag 1 and $\mathcal{E}^{2}_{j,t} = \phi_2\,(y_{j,t-2} - \mu_{j,t-2})$ at lag 2, with $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ shared across regions.

The base model is therefore:

\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
y_{j,t} &\sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{j,t} + \mathcal{E}^{1}_{j,t} + \mathcal{E}^{2}_{j,t},\ \tau_j^{-1}\right), \\
\mu_{j,t} &= \alpha_j + \mathcal{C}_{j,t}^{52} + \mathcal{R}_{j,t} + \mathbf{1}(j=\text{MW})\,\mathcal{MW}_t.
\end{aligned}
\label{eq:base-model}
\end{equation}

Secondary cycles are fitted as extensions: 1-month, 2-month, and 6-month cycles
($\mathcal{C}_{j,t}^{4}$, $\mathcal{C}_{j,t}^{8}$, and $\mathcal{C}_{j,t}^{26}$ respectively) in Section \@ref(sect:results-dic).

The addition of components is evaluated with the DIC: each step in Appendix \@ref(tab:app-dev-dic) lowers DIC, from `r round(read_csv(file.path("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k","v4.1","dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)` (regional level) to `r round(read_csv(file.path("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k","v4.6","dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)` (AR(2)). AR(2) is retained over AR(1) (`r round(read_csv(file.path("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k","v4.6","dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)` vs `r round(read_csv(file.path("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k","v4.5","dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)`).

## Bayesian Inference \label{sect:bayesian-inference}
The previous Section specified the mean structure and its components. What remains is to estimate the parameters with Bayesian inference. Recall: Bayesian inference treats every parameter as a random variable with its own distribution. Section \@ref(sect:likelihood) outlines the likelihood for the data. Section \@ref(sect:priors) introduces the prior, the distribution that represents uncertainty before seeing the data. Bayes theorem combines the likelihood, $p(y \mid \theta)$, and the prior, $p(\theta)$, into a posterior distribution, up to proportionality, 

$$
p(\theta \mid y) \propto p(y \mid \theta)\,p(\theta),
$$ 

which represents updated uncertainty. The posterior distributions for the parameters represent the uncertainty given the observed data model. This uncertainty is measured by default with 95% credible intervals, and the posterior mean is used as the point estimator.

### Likelihood \label{sect:likelihood}
The first part of the Bayes formula is the likelihood, which represents the probabilistic model for the data. Since the data is aggregated, summing days to weeks and hospitals into regions, the normality assumption becomes more reasonable due to the enlarged counts. The likelihood is therefore modelled as a normal distribution:
$$y_{j,t} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{j,t} + \mathcal{E}^{1}_{j,t} + \mathcal{E}^{2}_{j,t},\ \tau_j^{-1}\right), \quad t > 2,$$
with the lag-1 term only at $t=2$ and no autoregressive term at $t=1$. Here $\mu_{j,t}$ and the autoregressive terms are the mean structure developed in Section \@ref(sect:model-development). The regional precision is $\tau_j$.

### Priors \label{sect:priors}
Priors play a role in the posterior distribution by specifying mean and uncertainty for the parameters before seeing the data. For instance, a subject matter expert might have a strong belief that the annual cycle has a specific amplitude and phase, and could then encode their expectation in a prior distribution. In the case of our report, diffuse priors are used because there is no strong prior belief about any of our parameters; it is preferred to have prior distributions that are guaranteed to play a minimal role in the posterior distributions. The diffuse priors for the parameters are as follows:

\[
\alpha_j,\; \beta_j,\; \gamma_j,\; \delta_{j,t},\; \kappa_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 0.001)
\]
\[
\tau_j \sim \Gamma(0.001,\; 0.001) \qquad \phi_1,\,\phi_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\; 0.01).
\]

The normal priors are written in the precision parameterisation used by JAGS, where the second argument is the precision. A precision of 0.001 corresponds to a variance of 1000, approximately equivalent to a standard deviation of $\approx 31.6$. Because our rates range from roughly 0 to 10, the above priors are diffuse. The AR coefficients priors are also diffuse, with a precision of 0.01 (standard deviation of 10). The $\Gamma(0.001, 0.001)$ prior on the precision is diffuse, with a mean of 1 and a standard deviation of $\approx 31.6$. The priors are the same across all regions and weeks, so they do not influence the ranking between regions.


### Posterior \label{sect:posterior}

Posterior sampling is performed using *Just Another Gibbs Sampler* (*JAGS*)[@Plummer2003], implemented by pyJAGS [@Nowotny2024pyjags], a Python interface to JAGS. Sampling uses 4 chains, each sampled independently. Each chain runs 30,000 iterations: the first 10,000 are discarded as burn-in, leaving 20,000 retained per chain. No thinning is applied. Pooling the 4 chains gives 80,000 posterior draws. Convergence is assessed using the Gelman-Rubin diagnostic and trace plots; all $R$-hat values were at or below 1.0002, with full diagnostics in Appendix \@ref(app:traces).

## Bayesian Ranking \label{sect:ranking}
Health region levels, $\alpha^s_j$, are used for ranking, where $j$ is one of the 6
HSE health regions, $j\in\{1,...,6\}$, and $s$ is the draw index, $s\in\{1,...,n\}$,
where the total number of pooled posterior draws is $n=80{,}000$ (4 chains $\times$ 20,000 retained).
$$
  A =
  \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccc}
    \alpha_1^1 & \cdots & \alpha_j^1 & \cdots & \alpha_6^1\\
    \vdots  &        & \vdots\\
    \alpha_1^s & \cdots & \alpha_j^s& \cdots & \alpha_6^s\\
    \vdots  &        & \vdots\\
    \alpha_1^n & \cdots & \alpha_j^n & \cdots & \alpha_6^n\\
  \end{array} } \right].
$$
Ranks follow the posterior ranking approach of @Laird1989. Each
posterior sample is ranked to form the rank distribution, $R$:
$$
  R =
  \left[ {\begin{array}{ccccc}
    r_1^1 & \cdots & r_j^1 & \cdots & r_6^1\\
    \vdots  &        & \vdots\\
    r_1^s & \cdots & r_j^s& \cdots & r_6^s\\
    \vdots  &        & \vdots\\
    r_1^n & \cdots & r_j^n & \cdots & r_6^n\\
  \end{array} } \right],
$$
where $r_j^s=\text{rank}(\alpha_j^s)=\sum^6_{i=1}I(\alpha_j^s>\alpha_i^s)$.
When a tie occurs, the average rank is assigned to the tied values.

The posterior mean rank, $\hat{r}_j$, is calculated by taking the mean across the $n$ posterior samples for each region $j$:

$$
\hat{r}_j = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{s=1}^{n} r_j^s.
$$

Collecting the mean ranks across the six regions gives the vector:
$$
  \hat{R} =
  \left(\hat{r}_1, \hat{r}_2, \hat{r}_3, \hat{r}_4, \hat{r}_5,  \hat{r}_6 \right).
$$
From which the posterior mean rank is ranked, resulting in the ranked mean.
$$
  R^* =
    \left(r^*_1, r^*_2, r^*_3, r^*_4, r^*_5, r^*_6\right),
$$
where $r_j^*=rank(\hat{r}_j)=\sum^6_{i=1}I(\hat{r}_j>\hat{r}_i)$.

## Pairwise tests \label{sect:pairwise-tests}

The ranking orders the regions, but a pairwise test is needed to say whether any pair of regions actually differ on any parameter. Two regions, $j$ and $j'$, are compared on a parameter $\theta$ by subtracting their posteriors, where $j, j' \in \{1,\dots,6\}$ and $j \neq j'$. Within each draw the difference is $d_{j,j'}^s = \theta_j^s - \theta_{j'}^s$, and the $n$ values give the posterior of the difference, $d_{j,j'}$. Its 95% credible interval is the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of $d_{j,j'}^s$. An interval that excludes 0 means the two regions differ at the 95% level. No multiple-comparison correction is applied. The interval describes the difference itself, not a long-run error rate. This is applied to the regional level $\alpha_j$ (Section \@ref(sect:results-baselines)) and the annual cycle amplitude $A_j$ (Section \@ref(sect:results-cycle-amplitude)).


# Results \label{chap:results}
In this Section we explore the posterior samples of the parameters, quantities that are derived from those, and model fit diagnostics. The posterior samples are used to calculate the posterior mean and 95% credible intervals (CIs) for the parameters. The posterior mean is the average of the parameter values across all posterior samples, and represents a point estimate of the parameter. The 95% CI is calculated as the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the posterior distribution, and represents the range that has a 95% probability of containing the true parameter value, given the observed data, prior, and model. 

The model's mean component, composed of the level, annual cycle, New Year effect, and Mid West reset effect, is presented first. Followed by model extensions such as the 6, 2, and 1 month cycles. Posterior means, and 95% credible intervals (CIs) are shown below when applicable.

## Mean Component \label{sect:results-mean}

The mean component of the model, $\mu_{j,t}$, additively captures the regional level, annual cycle, New Year effect, and Mid West reset effect for each region and week. The level differences between regions are captured by the $\alpha_j$ parameters. They are apparent in the aggregate mean component, seen in Figure \@ref(fig:results-mu-ribbon), where the regions with higher levels have higher mean component values across the series.

The annual cycle term peaks in winter and troughs in summer for every region. The New Year effect, a block term, appears as the sharp dips spanning Christmas and the New Year. The Mid West reset, also a block term, appears as the isolated dip in the HSE Mid West component in August 2024. Posterior means and 95% credible intervals for each component are reported below.

```{r results-mu-ribbon, fig.cap="Posterior mean component by week. Colours indicate HSE regions; shaded bands are 95\\% credible intervals for the mean, $\\mu_{j,t}$.", fig.width=10, fig.height=3, out.width="100%"}
mu_dir <- selected_model
mu    <- read_csv(file.path(mu_dir, "mu.csv"),       show_col_types = FALSE)
mu_lo <- read_csv(file.path(mu_dir, "mu_lower.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
mu_hi <- read_csv(file.path(mu_dir, "mu_upper.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

dates <- as.Date(names(read_csv(
  "../data/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k.csv",
  n_max = 0, show_col_types = FALSE
))[-1])

to_long <- function(df, value_col) {
  df |>
    mutate(week_start = dates) |>
    pivot_longer(-week_start, names_to = "region", values_to = value_col)
}

mu_df <- to_long(mu, "mu") |>
  left_join(to_long(mu_lo, "lo"), by = c("week_start", "region")) |>
  left_join(to_long(mu_hi, "hi"), by = c("week_start", "region"))

ggplot(mu_df, aes(week_start, mu, colour = region, fill = region)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), colour = NA, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_palette) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = region_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Trolleys per 10,000 population", colour = NULL) +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```


### Regional Levels \label{sect:results-baselines}

```{r}
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
midwest_idx     <- idx_by_region[["HSE Mid West"]]
midwestposmean  <- round(mean(raw[[paste0("alpha[", midwest_idx, "]")]]), 2)
```
The regional levels, $\alpha_j$, capture the expected level for each region; they estimate the rate of the region ignoring any time varying effects. Level parameters are of interest for ranking, as they capture the underlying level for each region, controlling for the other components in the model.
```{r results-alpha-map, fig.cap="Regional Levels (\\(\\alpha_j\\)) per 10,000 population.", fig.width=6, fig.height=6, out.width="55%"}
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
regions <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands", "HSE Dublin and North East",
  "HSE Dublin and South East", "HSE Mid West",
  "HSE South West", "HSE West and North West"
)
alpha_means <- tibble(
  region = regions,
  alpha  = sapply(seq_along(regions),
                  function(i) mean(raw[[paste0("alpha[", i, "]")]])),
  lo     = sapply(seq_along(regions),
                  function(i) quantile(raw[[paste0("alpha[", i, "]")]], 0.025)),
  hi     = sapply(seq_along(regions),
                  function(i) quantile(raw[[paste0("alpha[", i, "]")]], 0.975))
)

name_map <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands HR"   = "HSE Dublin and Midlands",
  "HSE Dublin and North East HR" = "HSE Dublin and North East",
  "HSE Dublin and South East HR" = "HSE Dublin and South East",
  "HSE Midwest HR"               = "HSE Mid West",
  "HSE South West HR"            = "HSE South West",
  "HSE West and North West HR"   = "HSE West and North West"
)
shp <- sf::read_sf("../data/mapping/hse_regions.geojson") |>
  mutate(region = name_map[HR_operational_name]) |>
  left_join(alpha_means, by = "region")

ni <- rnaturalearth::ne_states(country = "united kingdom", returnclass = "sf") |>
  dplyr::filter(geonunit == "Northern Ireland") |>
  sf::st_transform(sf::st_crs(shp))

ie_counties <- rnaturalearth::ne_states(country = "Ireland", returnclass = "sf") |>
  sf::st_transform(sf::st_crs(shp))

wnw_label  <- shp |> dplyr::filter(region == "HSE West and North West")
other_label <- shp |> dplyr::filter(region != "HSE West and North West")

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = ni, fill = "grey92", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = shp, aes(fill = alpha), colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_sf(data = ie_counties, fill = NA, colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.15) +
  geom_sf_label(data = other_label,
                aes(label = sprintf("%s\n%.2f [%.2f, %.2f]", region, alpha, lo, hi)),
                size = 2.6, fill = "white", label.size = 0,
                label.padding = unit(0.15, "lines")) +
  geom_sf_label(data = wnw_label,
                aes(label = sprintf("%s\n%.2f [%.2f, %.2f]", region, alpha, lo, hi)),
                size = 2.6, fill = "white", label.size = 0,
                label.padding = unit(0.15, "lines"),
                nudge_x = -0.3, nudge_y = -0.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#f7fcf5", high = "#005a32",
                      name = expression(alpha)) +
  theme_void(base_size = 11)
```

Across regions, the level posterior mean rate per 10,000 population is highest in HSE Mid West (`r midwestposmean`, 95% CI [`r round(quantile(raw[[paste0("alpha[", midwest_idx, "]")]], 0.025), 2)`, `r round(quantile(raw[[paste0("alpha[", midwest_idx, "]")]], 0.975), 2)`]) and lowest in HSE Dublin and North East (`r round(mean(raw[[paste0("alpha[", idx_by_region[["HSE Dublin and North East"]], "]")]]), 2)`, 95% CI [`r round(quantile(raw[[paste0("alpha[", idx_by_region[["HSE Dublin and North East"]], "]")]], 0.025), 2)`, `r round(quantile(raw[[paste0("alpha[", idx_by_region[["HSE Dublin and North East"]], "]")]], 0.975), 2)`]). All but one pairwise difference between regional levels excludes zero (Appendix Table \@ref(tab:results-alpha-pairwise)); the exception is HSE South West and HSE Dublin and Midlands, whose levels are not credibly separated. The ordering is otherwise confident. In particular, three western regions (HSE Mid West, HSE South West, HSE West and North West) all have higher levels than the three eastern regions (HSE Dublin and Midlands, HSE Dublin and North East, HSE Dublin and South East), consistent with an East-West divide in demand and service provision. Figure \@ref(fig:results-alpha-map) shows the spatial distribution of regional levels, visually confirming East-West differences. Because the levels separate cleanly, their ordering is stable. In the next section this is formalised with the rank distribution.

```{r east-west-setup, include=FALSE}
east_regions <- c("HSE Dublin and Midlands",
                  "HSE Dublin and North East",
                  "HSE Dublin and South East")
west_regions <- c("HSE Mid West",
                  "HSE South West",
                  "HSE West and North West")
east_draws <- rowSums(sapply(east_regions,
                             function(r) raw[[paste0("alpha[", idx_by_region[[r]], "]")]]))
west_draws <- rowSums(sapply(west_regions,
                             function(r) raw[[paste0("alpha[", idx_by_region[[r]], "]")]]))
diff_draws <- west_draws - east_draws
```

```{r alpha-helpers, include=FALSE}
alpha_ci <- function(i) {
  col <- paste0("alpha[", i, "]")
  sprintf("%.2f [%.2f, %.2f]",
          mean(raw[[col]]),
          quantile(raw[[col]], 0.025),
          quantile(raw[[col]], 0.975))
}
```



## Regional Rankings \label{sect:results-rank}

The rank distribution follows from Section \@ref(sect:ranking) which describes the ranking process. The primary scaling is per 10,000 population; a sensitivity analysis refits the model under three alternative denominators: per 100 inpatient beds, per €1 billion of regional budget, and per 1,000 population aged 65+ to more fully explore the factors impacting the on trolley rates.

### Per 10,000 Population

For four of the six regions the mean rank, median rank, ranked mean, and 95% CI coincide on a single integer (Table \@ref(tab:results-rank-table)). HSE South West and HSE Dublin and Midlands are the exception, each with a 95% rank CI of [3, 4], swapping ranks 3 and 4 in about 3% of posterior draws. The ordering is HSE Mid West (1), HSE West and North West (2), HSE South West and HSE Dublin and Midlands (ranks 3--4), HSE Dublin and South East (5), HSE Dublin and North East (6). Per 10,000 population still yields the clearest ranking among the four denominators considered, with only the adjacent South West and Dublin and Midlands positions unresolved.

### Sensitivity to Scaling \label{sect:results-sensitivity}

The ranking is broadly robust to scale choice (Table \@ref(tab:results-rank-table)). All four scalings agree on HSE Mid West first, HSE West and North West second, and the two Dublin regions at the bottom (HSE Dublin and South East 5th, HSE Dublin and North East 6th). The middle two positions are less stable. Under per €1 billion of regional budget scaling, HSE Dublin and Midlands and HSE Dublin and South East have rank CIs of [4, 5]. Under per 1,000 population aged 65+ scaling, HSE Dublin and Midlands and HSE South West swap into ranks 3 and 4 respectively, both with CIs [3, 4].

```{r results-rank-table}
regions <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands", "HSE Dublin and North East",
  "HSE Dublin and South East", "HSE Mid West",
  "HSE South West", "HSE West and North West"
)

scaling_paths <- list(
  "Per 10,000 population"             = "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k/v4.6/raw_samples.csv",
  "Per 100 inpatient beds"            = "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPerBed/v4.6/raw_samples.csv",
  "Per €1 billion of regional budget" = "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPerBudgetThousands/v4.6/raw_samples.csv",
  "Per 1,000 population aged 65+"     = "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer1kOver65/v4.6/raw_samples.csv"
)

rank_section <- function(path) {
  raw <- read_csv(path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  alpha_mat <- sapply(seq_along(regions),
                      function(i) raw[[paste0("alpha[", i, "]")]])
  ranks_mat <- t(apply(alpha_mat, 1,
                       function(row) rank(-row, ties.method = "average")))
  colnames(ranks_mat) <- regions
  mean_rank   <- colMeans(ranks_mat)
  ranked_mean <- rank(mean_rank, ties.method = "average")
  p_off_rank  <- sapply(seq_along(regions),
                        function(j) mean(ranks_mat[, j] != ranked_mean[j]))
  tibble(
    Region        = regions,
    `Ranked Mean` = ranked_mean,
    `Mean Rank`   = mean_rank,
    `Median Rank` = apply(ranks_mat, 2, median),
    Lo            = apply(ranks_mat, 2, quantile, 0.025),
    Hi            = apply(ranks_mat, 2, quantile, 0.975),
    `P(off rank)` = p_off_rank
  ) |>
    mutate(
      `95% CI`      = sprintf("[%g, %g]", Lo, Hi),
      `Mean Rank`   = sprintf("%.1f", `Mean Rank`),
      `Median Rank` = sprintf("%.1f", `Median Rank`),
      `P(off rank)` = ifelse(`P(off rank)` < 0.001, "<0.001", sprintf("%.3f", `P(off rank)`))
    ) |>
    arrange(Region) |>
    select(Region, `Ranked Mean`, `Mean Rank`, `Median Rank`, `95% CI`, `P(off rank)`)
}

combined <- bind_rows(lapply(scaling_paths, rank_section))

tbl <- kableExtra::kbl(combined, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, linesep = "",
                       align = c("l", "c", "c", "c", "c", "r"),
                       caption = "Posterior ranks (1 = highest alpha) across response scalings. Consistent ranks across sections indicate the ordering is robust to denominator choice. P(off rank) is the posterior probability that a draw places the region at a rank other than its Ranked Mean.")
scaling_names <- names(scaling_paths)
for (i in seq_along(scaling_names)) {
  tbl <- kableExtra::pack_rows(tbl, scaling_names[i],
                               (i - 1) * 6 + 1, i * 6)
}
kableExtra::kable_styling(tbl, latex_options = "repeat_header")
```


## Annual Cycle \label{sect:results-cycle}
In this section, the annual cycle is analysed in terms of its phase and amplitude. The phase captures the timing of the cycle, while the amplitude captures the strength of the cycle. Both are derived from the cosine and sine coefficients of the annual cycle, $\beta_j$ and $\gamma_j$, as described in Section \@ref(sect:model-development). This section continues to use the primary scaling of per 10,000 population. Sensitivity analyses for the annual cycle are not performed, as the cycle is a relatively small effect that should be less sensitive to scaling than the level and ranking.

### Annual Cycle Phase \label{sect:results-cycle-phase}
```{r results-phase-inline, include=FALSE}
# Phase stats are computed by thesis/phase_table.py -> phase_table.csv
phase_tab <- read_csv("phase_table.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
regions <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands", "HSE Dublin and North East",
  "HSE Dublin and South East", "HSE Mid West",
  "HSE South West", "HSE West and North West"
)
phase_row   <- function(i) phase_tab[match(regions[i], phase_tab$region), ]
phase_val   <- function(i) {
  r <- phase_row(i)
  sprintf("%+.1f wk, 95%% CI [%+.1f, %+.1f]", r$mean_pk, r$ci_lo, r$ci_hi)
}
phase_width <- function(i) sprintf("%.1f", phase_row(i)$width)
phase_prob  <- function(i) sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * phase_row(i)$p_winter)
```
Four of six regions have 95% CI widths narrower than 3 months: HSE Dublin and
Midlands (`r phase_width(1)` weeks), HSE Dublin and North East (`r phase_width(2)` weeks),
HSE Dublin and South East (`r phase_width(3)` weeks), and HSE South West
(`r phase_width(5)` weeks). HSE West and North West (`r phase_width(6)` weeks) and
HSE Mid West (`r phase_width(4)` weeks) are wider. The four narrow CIs cluster
close to the New Year, seen in Figure \@ref(fig:results-phase-plot), indicating a
consistent annual cycle with winter peaks.

Five of the six regions place most of their posterior mass on a peak in winter, where winter spans December to February [@MetEireann_Climate]. The winter peak is near-certain for HSE Dublin and North East (`r phase_prob(2)`), HSE Dublin and South East (`r phase_prob(3)`), and HSE South West (`r phase_prob(5)`), and clear but less concentrated for HSE Dublin and Midlands (`r phase_prob(1)`) and HSE West and North West (`r phase_prob(6)`). HSE Mid West (`r phase_prob(4)`) is the exception, with its peak poorly identified, a wide credible interval, and less pronounced amplitude (Table \@ref(tab:results-amplitude-table)). Credible intervals, probabilities, and posterior means can be found in Appendix Table \@ref(tab:results-phase-table).

```{r results-phase-plot, fig.cap="Peak phases plot. Shaded blue region represents winter (1st of December to end of February). Black points are the regional posterior means. Bars are the 95% credible interval.", fig.width=8, fig.height=4}
# raw is loaded here for the amplitude inline that follows this chunk;
# phase stats come from thesis/phase_table.py -> phase_table.csv
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
phase_tab <- read_csv("phase_table.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

xmax <- ceiling(max(abs(c(phase_tab$ci_lo, phase_tab$ci_hi))) / 4) * 4

ggplot(phase_tab,
       aes(y = factor(region, levels = rev(phase_tab$region)))) +
  # Meteorological winter (Met Eireann): Dec 1 to end of Feb, relative to New Year.
  # Dec 1 = -31 days = -31/7 weeks; end of Feb = +59 days = +59/7 weeks.
  annotate("rect", xmin = -31/7, xmax = 59/7, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_pointrange(aes(x = mean_pk, xmin = ci_lo, xmax = ci_hi)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-xmax, xmax, 4), limits = c(-xmax, xmax)) +
  labs(x = "Weeks from New Year (0 = year change)", y = NULL) +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11)
```


### Annual Cycle Amplitude \label{sect:results-cycle-amplitude}

The posterior means of the amplitude range from 0.62 in HSE Mid West to 1.24 in
HSE South West. The amplitude posterior means add a large trolley burden to
some regions. For instance, the cycle in HSE Dublin and North East swings
`r {r2 <- sqrt(raw[["beta[2]"]]^2 + raw[["gamma[2]"]]^2) / raw[["alpha[2]"]]; q <- quantile(r2, c(0.025, 0.975)); sprintf("±%.0f%% (95%% CI [±%.0f%%, ±%.0f%%])", 100*mean(r2), 100*q[1], 100*q[2])}`
around the regional level: winter peaks sit that fraction above the regional mean and
summer troughs sit the same fraction below. All 95% credible intervals are wide.
The pairwise test in Appendix Table \@ref(tab:results-amplitude-pairwise) shows the
regions are not comparable as all region combinations have a 95% CI which
contains 0.

The model comparison agrees. A single shared cycle has a slightly lower DIC than the region-specific cycle (Appendix Table \@ref(tab:app-regional-structure-dic)). The regions differ little in their annual cycle. The amplitudes are not separated and five of six phases cluster near the winter peak. HSE Mid West is the exception, with the widest phase interval and the least pronounced amplitude, so its annual cycle cannot be resolved.

```{r results-amplitude-table}
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
regions <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands", "HSE Dublin and North East",
  "HSE Dublin and South East", "HSE Mid West",
  "HSE South West", "HSE West and North West"
)

amp_tab <- bind_rows(lapply(seq_along(regions), function(i) {
  A <- sqrt(raw[[paste0("beta[", i, "]")]]^2 +
            raw[[paste0("gamma[", i, "]")]]^2)
  q <- quantile(A, c(0.025, 0.975))
  tibble(
    Region       = regions[i],
    Mean         = mean(A),
    `95% CI`     = sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]", q[1], q[2]),
    `Contains 0` = ifelse(q[1] <= 0 & q[2] >= 0, "Yes", "No")
  )
})) |> arrange(Region)

knitr::kable(amp_tab, digits = 2, booktabs = TRUE, linesep = "", caption = "annual cycle amplitude.")
```


## New Year Effect \label{sect:results-ny}

```{r ny-helpers, include=FALSE}
ny_ci <- function(i, period = "mid") {
  col <- paste0("delta_", period, "[", i, "]")
  sprintf("%.2f [%.2f, %.2f]",
          mean(raw[[col]]),
          quantile(raw[[col]], 0.025),
          quantile(raw[[col]], 0.975))
}
```

Based on Figure \@ref(fig:results-ny-effect), the New Year effect
on all regions has a roughly similar shape. HSE Dublin and North East and HSE Dublin
and South East have the narrowest CIs, suggesting their New Year effects
are the most consistent across observed years. During the New Year
week, regions differ in whether their credible intervals return to the regional
level. Some include it, others exclude it. For instance HSE South West (`r ny_ci(5)`),
and HSE West and North West (`r ny_ci(6)`) return to the regional level. One week after the
New Year, HSE Dublin and North East (`r ny_ci(2, "post")`), HSE South West
(`r ny_ci(5, "post")`), and HSE West and North West (`r ny_ci(6, "post")`) have
CI lower bounds above the level. The remaining three span the level. HSE West
and North West has the highest posterior mean.

```{r results-ny-effect, fig.cap="New Year effect by region. Points are posterior means; bars are 95\\% credible intervals. Each point is annotated with its posterior mean and 95\\% CI. Dashed line at zero, representing the regional level", fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
regions <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands", "HSE Dublin and North East",
  "HSE Dublin and South East", "HSE Mid West",
  "HSE South West", "HSE West and North West"
)
periods <- c("NY-2", "NY-1", "NY", "NY+1")
params  <- c("delta_pm1", "delta_pre", "delta_mid", "delta_post")

ny_all <- bind_rows(lapply(seq_along(regions), function(i) {
  bind_rows(lapply(seq_along(periods), function(j) {
    col <- paste0(params[j], "[", i, "]")
    tibble(
      Region = regions[i],
      Period = factor(periods[j], levels = periods),
      Mean   = mean(raw[[col]]),
      Lo     = quantile(raw[[col]], 0.025),
      Hi     = quantile(raw[[col]], 0.975)
    )
  }))
})) |>
  mutate(Label = sprintf("%.2f\n[%.2f, %.2f]", Mean, Lo, Hi))

ggplot(ny_all, aes(Period, Mean)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = Lo, ymax = Hi), colour = "#1f77b4") +
  geom_line(aes(group = 1), colour = "#1f77b4") +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(aes(label = Label, y = Hi), size = 2.2,
                           lineheight = 0.85, colour = "grey25",
                           segment.colour = "grey75", min.segment.length = 0,
                           box.padding = 0.3, nudge_y = 0.9, direction = "y",
                           seed = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ Region, ncol = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.16))) +
  labs(x = "NY period", y = "Trolleys per 10,000 population") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11)
```


## Mid West Reset \label{sect:results-mw}

```{r mw-helpers, include=FALSE}
mw_ci <- function(w) {
  col <- paste0("sigma_w", w)
  sprintf("%.2f [%.2f, %.2f]",
          mean(raw[[col]]),
          quantile(raw[[col]], 0.025),
          quantile(raw[[col]], 0.975))
}
```

The HSE Mid West deferral period runs from the 8th to the 18th of August 2024,
falling within the weeks of the 5th, `r mw_ci(1)`, and the 12th of August,
`r mw_ci(2)`, shown in Figure \@ref(fig:results-mw-reset).
By posterior mean, the minimum is the week of the 19th of August, `r mw_ci(3)`.
The week of the 2nd of September, `r mw_ci(5)`, shows a return to the regional level.


```{r results-mw-reset, fig.cap="Mid West reset effect for the five weeks from the 5th of August 2024. Points are posterior means, bars are 95\\% credible intervals, with each point annotated with its mean and 95\\% CI. Dashed line at zero.", fig.width=7, fig.height=2.8}
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
mw_tab <- tibble(
  Week  = factor(c("W1\n(Aug 5)", "W2\n(Aug 12)", "W3\n(Aug 19)",
                   "W4\n(Aug 26)", "W5\n(Sep 2)"),
                 levels = c("W1\n(Aug 5)", "W2\n(Aug 12)", "W3\n(Aug 19)",
                            "W4\n(Aug 26)", "W5\n(Sep 2)")),
  Phase = c("reset", "reset", "post", "post", "post"),
  col   = paste0("sigma_w", 1:5)
) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    Mean = mean(raw[[col]]),
    Lo   = quantile(raw[[col]], 0.025),
    Hi   = quantile(raw[[col]], 0.975)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    Label = sprintf("%.2f\n[%.2f, %.2f]", Mean, Lo, Hi),
    # W1 label sits below its CI; the rest above, W3 pushed highest
    LabY  = if_else(row_number() == 1, Lo, Hi),
    Nudge = c(-1.6, 0.9, 2.6, 0.9, 0.9)
  )

ggplot(mw_tab, aes(Week, Mean)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_pointrange(aes(ymin = Lo, ymax = Hi), colour = "#1f77b4") +
  geom_line(aes(group = 1), colour = "#1f77b4") +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(aes(label = Label, y = LabY), size = 2.6,
                           lineheight = 0.85, colour = "grey25",
                           segment.colour = "grey75", min.segment.length = 0,
                           box.padding = 0.4, nudge_y = mw_tab$Nudge,
                           direction = "y", seed = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.12, 0.10))) +
  labs(x = "Week (start date, 2024)",
       y = "Effect (weekly trolleys per 10,000 population)") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11)
```

## Model Extensions \label{sect:results-dic}

The base model in Equation \@ref(eq:base-model) is extended with three secondary cycles: a 4 week cycle, an 8 week cycle, and a 26 week cycle. As discussed in the spectral analysis (Section \@ref(sect:data-EDA-PSD)), the periodogram in Figure \@ref(fig:eda-psd) shows elevated power near the 4, 8, and 26 week periods across regions; the extensions are specified in Section \@ref(sect:model-development). By Table \@ref(tab:results-dic-table), the 4 and 8 week cycles decrease deviance but increase DIC; the components fit better but add too much complexity. The 26 week cycle increases both deviance and DIC; meaning the additional component worsens fit. None of the cycles lower DIC below the base model. AR(2) was selected as the base over AR(1) on the same criterion (DIC `r round(read_csv(file.path(selected_model, "dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)` vs `r round(read_csv(file.path(ar1_model, "dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)`); the full development sequence is in Appendix \@ref(tab:app-dev-dic).

```{r results-dic-table}
base   <- "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k"
labels <- c(
  v4.6 = "Base model",
  v4.7 = "Base + 4 week cycle",
  v4.8 = "Base + 8 week cycle",
  v4.9 = "Base + 26 week cycle"
)
dic_tab <- bind_rows(lapply(names(labels), function(v) {
  d <- read_csv(file.path(base, v, "dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
  tibble(
    Description = labels[[v]],
    Deviance    = d$deviance,
    pD          = d$penalty,
    DIC         = d$DIC
  )
