}))

knitr::kable(dic_tab, digits = 1, booktabs = TRUE, linesep = "", caption = "Cycle extensions added to the base model (per 10,000 population).")
```



## Residuals \label{sect:results-resid}

The residual is the gap between the observation and the full one-step prediction, $r_{j,t} = y_{j,t} - (\mu_{j,t} + \mathcal{E}^{1}_{j,t} + \mathcal{E}^{2}_{j,t})$. Since the observation variance is $\tau_j^{-1}$, dividing by the standard deviation, $\tau_j^{-1/2}$, gives the standardised residual
$$z_{j,t} = r_{j,t}\sqrt{\tau_j}.$$
Under a well-specified model the residuals already have mean 0. Dividing by the standard deviation $\tau_j^{-1/2}$ is what brings them to variance 1, on a common scale across regions.

Shown in Figure \@ref(fig:results-resid), the model residuals are not white noise. There are clear cycles, however, the 4, 8 and 26 week cycles tested in Section \@ref(sect:results-dic) do not improve model fit as measured by the DIC. There is visible regional dependence which can be seen when regions move in step; appearing to synchronise. This is shown to be meaningful in the following section, \@ref(sect:results-ccf).

```{r results-resid, fig.cap="Standardised posterior mean residuals from the selected model, by week. Colour indicates region.", fig.width=9, fig.height=3}
res <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "residuals_posterior_mean.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
regions <- names(res)
tau_mean <- sapply(seq_along(regions),
                   function(i) mean(raw[[paste0("tau[", i, "]")]]))
dates <- as.Date(names(read_csv(
  "../data/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k.csv",
  n_max = 0, show_col_types = FALSE
))[-1])

resid_long <- res |>
  mutate(week_start = dates) |>
  pivot_longer(-week_start, names_to = "region", values_to = "r") |>
  mutate(
    tau = tau_mean[match(region, regions)],
    std = r * sqrt(tau)
  )

ggplot(resid_long, aes(week_start, std, colour = region)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_palette) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Standardised residual", colour = NULL) +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


### Cross-Correlation \label{sect:results-ccf}

Figure \@ref(fig:results-ccf) (below) shows a heatmap of 0-lags of the cross-correlation function (CCF) results of residual region pairs. The CCF works by extending the ACF, $\rho_{xy}(k) = \operatorname{corr}(x_t,\, y_{t-k})$, where $x$ is one region's residual signal and y is another. All 15 region pairs have a cross-correlation at lag-0 exceeding the significance band, which provides evidence for a regional dependence structure.

```{r results-ccf, fig.cap="Lag-0 cross-correlation of posterior mean residuals for all 15 HSE region pairs. Darker blue indicates a stronger positive correlation. An asterisk marks pairs above the 95\\% significance band ($|r| > 0.15$).", fig.width=8, fig.height=4.6, out.width="50%"}
res <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "residuals_posterior_mean.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
labs_wrap <- c("HSE Dublin and Midlands"   = "Dublin and\nMidlands",
               "HSE Dublin and North East" = "Dublin and\nNorth East",
               "HSE Dublin and South East" = "Dublin and\nSouth East",
               "HSE Mid West"              = "Mid West",
               "HSE South West"            = "South West",
               "HSE West and North West"   = "West and\nNorth West")
ord  <- unname(labs_wrap[names(res)])
band <- qnorm(0.975) / sqrt(nrow(res))

# ranked, de-duplicated pair list (clean region names) for the prose below
ccf_pairs <- cor(res) |>
  `dimnames<-`(list(sub("^HSE ", "", names(res)), sub("^HSE ", "", names(res)))) |>
  as.table() |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  setNames(c("a", "b", "r")) |>
  filter(as.character(a) < as.character(b)) |>
  arrange(desc(r))

# lookup for a single named pair, for inline use in the prose
ccf_r <- function(x, y) {
  m <- cor(res)
  dimnames(m) <- list(sub("^HSE ", "", names(res)), sub("^HSE ", "", names(res)))
  sprintf("%.2f", m[x, y])
}

cmat <- cor(res)
rownames(cmat) <- colnames(cmat) <- ord
cmat[upper.tri(cmat, diag = TRUE)] <- NA

cor_df <- as.data.frame(as.table(cmat)) |>
  setNames(c("row", "col", "r")) |>
  filter(!is.na(r)) |>
  mutate(row   = factor(row, levels = rev(ord)),
         col   = factor(col, levels = ord),
         label = sprintf("%.2f%s", r, ifelse(abs(r) > band, "*", "")),
         txt   = ifelse(r >= 0.26, "white", "grey15"))

ggplot(cor_df, aes(col, row, fill = r)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label, colour = txt), size = 2.7) +
  scale_colour_identity() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#eef3f8", high = "#2166ac",
                      limits = c(0, NA), name = "Lag-0\ncross-corr. (r)",
                      guide = guide_colourbar(
                        barwidth = grid::unit(0.5, "lines"),
                        barheight = grid::unit(0.33, "npc"))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL,
       caption = sprintf("* marks pairs with |r| > %.2f (95%% significance band)", band)) +
  coord_equal() +
  theme_thesis(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = c(0.85, 0.72),
        legend.title = element_text(size = 9, lineheight = 0.95),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
        axis.text = element_text(colour = "grey30"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, colour = "grey40", hjust = 0,
                                    margin = margin(t = 6)),
        plot.margin = margin(t = 8, r = 6, b = 4, l = 4))
```

We expect regional dependence structure to be driven by demographics like age, with Dublin and non-Dublin regions clustering separately. The data only partly supports this. The three strongest pairs are `r ccf_pairs$a[1]` with `r ccf_pairs$b[1]` (`r sprintf("%.2f", ccf_pairs$r[1])`), `r ccf_pairs$a[2]` with `r ccf_pairs$b[2]` (`r sprintf("%.2f", ccf_pairs$r[2])`), and `r ccf_pairs$a[3]` with `r ccf_pairs$b[3]` (`r sprintf("%.2f", ccf_pairs$r[3])`). Two of the three cross the Dublin and non-Dublin boundary, so the dependence structure does not split cleanly along Dublin lines. We would also expect weaker correlations between regions that do not share a border, however, HSE South West and West and North West have a moderate correlation (`r ccf_r("South West","West and North West")`) despite sharing no border. The reverse also holds. South West correlates least with its actual neighbours, Mid West (`r ccf_r("South West","Mid West")`) and Dublin and South East (`r ccf_r("South West","Dublin and South East")`), the two weakest links in the matrix. Geographic adjacency does not explain the dependence structure.

# Discussion \label{chap:Discussion}

```{r discussion-setup, include=FALSE}
raw <- read_csv(file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
dic_ar2 <- round(read_csv("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k/v4.6/dic.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)
dic_ar1 <- round(read_csv("../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k/v4.5/dic.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC, 1)
phi2_str <- sprintf("%.2f, 95%% CI [%.2f, %.2f]",
                    mean(raw[["phi2"]]),
                    quantile(raw[["phi2"]], 0.025),
                    quantile(raw[["phi2"]], 0.975))
```

This study applies a Bayesian time series regression framework to weekly hospital trolley rates across the six HSE health regions of Ireland, with the primary aim of comparing regional burden after controlling for population size, seasonality, and known operational events. The regression component, capturing regional levels, an annual cycle, New Year effects, and a Mid West operational reset, is the central focus of this analysis. The autoregressive term serves a secondary role, accounting for residual week-to-week persistence in the data rather than being of substantive interest in itself. An AR(2) term is selected: it gives the lowest DIC (`r dic_ar2` against `r dic_ar1` for the AR(1) specification), and the lag-2 coefficient $\phi_2$ is credibly non-zero (`r phi2_str`). Three further extensions, a 4-week cycle, an 8-week cycle, and a 26-week cycle, were tested and not adopted, as all worsened DIC. The selected model therefore comprises regional levels, an annual cycle, region-specific New Year effects, a Mid West operational reset, and an AR(2) persistence term.

The most substantive finding is the magnitude and credibility of regional differences in trolley pressure levels. The level posterior mean rate per 10,000 population is highest in HSE Mid West (`r alpha_ci(idx_by_region[["HSE Mid West"]])`) and lowest in HSE Dublin and North East (`r alpha_ci(idx_by_region[["HSE Dublin and North East"]])`). The pairwise comparison of posterior level distributions reveals near-certain regional orderings: for almost all region pairs, the posterior probability that one region has a higher level than another is either effectively 0 or 1, indicating the estimated ordering is not an artefact of posterior uncertainty. Fourteen of the fifteen pairwise credible intervals exclude zero (Appendix Table \@ref(tab:results-alpha-pairwise)); the single exception is HSE South West and HSE Dublin and Midlands, whose levels are not credibly separated. A clear west/east divide is evident, with the three western regions consistently showing substantially higher rates than the three Dublin regions. This divide is invisible in raw regional totals (Figure \@ref(fig:app-raw-boxplot)) because larger regions, particularly the Dublin regions, have substantially larger populations, producing higher absolute counts that mask the underlying per-capita pressure. Population standardisation is therefore essential for any meaningful comparison across regions of different sizes within a nationally administered health system.

The west/east divide likely reflects structural features of the Irish health system and population geography. HSE Mid West, the highest-burden region, covers a large and predominantly rural catchment spanning counties Limerick, Clare, and Tipperary, with acute hospital services concentrated in a small number of centres. The spatial distribution of the population across this region, combined with relatively limited acute capacity relative to catchment size, creates conditions where patients are more likely to experience delays in admission. A similar dynamic applies, to varying degrees, across the other western regions. Dublin regions, by contrast, benefit from a higher concentration of acute bed capacity relative to their populations, which may facilitate more rapid patient flow through emergency departments.

The formal Bayesian ranking of regions by their level, derived from the posterior samples of the regional levels in the spirit of @Laird1989, yields a stable and near-deterministic ordering. HSE Dublin and North East ranks lowest in burden and HSE Mid West highest, with negligible posterior standard deviation on the rank estimates. Under the primary per 10,000 population scaling, rank credible intervals collapse to a single integer for four of the six regions. The exception is the adjacent pair HSE South West and HSE Dublin and Midlands, which swap ranks 3 and 4 in about 3% of draws, each with a 95% CI of [3, 4]. This ordering is robust across all four scaling denominators examined: per 10,000 population, per 100 inpatient beds, per €1 billion of regional budget, and per 1,000 population aged 65+. The same South West and Dublin and Midlands pair again carries a 95% CI of [3, 4] under the age-structure scaling, confining the instability to these adjacent middle positions. The stability of the top two and bottom two positions across all denominators reinforces confidence in the principal finding.

An annual cycle is identified across all six regions, with five of six placing substantial posterior mass on a winter peak within two months of New Year (Figure \@ref(fig:results-phase-plot), Appendix Table \@ref(tab:results-phase-table)). This is consistent with established patterns of respiratory illness demand in Ireland and internationally, where cold weather and increased viral transmission drive hospital admissions in the winter months. HSE Mid West is the notable outlier, with only `r phase_prob(4)` of posterior mass in the winter window and a wide 95% CI on peak phase spanning `r phase_width(4)` weeks. This less clearly defined, low amplitude cycle in the Mid West may reflect the influence of the operational reset period in August 2024 on the estimated cycle for that region. Regional differences in cycle amplitude, while suggestive, cannot be credibly distinguished given the available data, as all pairwise amplitude comparisons contain zero (Appendix Table \@ref(tab:results-amplitude-pairwise)).

The New Year dip is identified across all regions and is consistent with the known HSE practice of discharging patients during the Christmas period in anticipation of the January surge in respiratory illness demand. The region-specific New Year effect, which allows each region to respond differently across the four-week window, captures genuine heterogeneity in the timing and magnitude of this effect that a global specification could not accommodate. The subsequent rebound above the regional level in several regions in the week after New Year reflects the rapid return of demand once the holiday period ends. The HSE Mid West operational reset in August 2024 produces a clear and localised dip in trolley rates, reaching a minimum in the week of the 19th of August (`r mw_ci(3)` per 10,000), with return to the regional level by early September. The effect is clinically plausible and statistically well identified, with credible intervals excluding zero across the reset weeks. The absence of any comparable pattern in the other five regions over the same period confirms this is a region-specific effect attributable to the operational intervention rather than a seasonal or system-wide phenomenon.

The standardised residuals are approximately normal across regions, with only mild heavy tails in HSE Dublin and South East, supporting the Normal likelihood (Appendix Figures \@ref(fig:app-resid-qq), \@ref(fig:app-resid-hist)). Residual diagnostics indicate that some sub-annual structure and cross-regional dependence remain unexplained by the selected model (Appendix Figures \@ref(fig:app-resid-psd) and \@ref(fig:app-resid-pacf)). Significant cross-correlations at lag zero are observed across all 15 region pairs, suggesting that some shared temporal variation is not captured by the current independent-regions specification.

# Limitations and Future Work \label{chap:limitations}

The model operates on weekly aggregated trolley rates, which smooths over the pronounced daily variation visible in the partial autocorrelation function. A model operating at daily resolution with an explicit within-week cycle would provide a more granular characterisation of hospital pressure dynamics and may be of greater operational relevance to healthcare managers. Weekly binning also splits the New Year holiday unevenly, as the 1st of January falls on a different weekday each year (Section \@ref(sect:data-EDA-nydip)), so daily counts would give more observations to identify the New Year and Mid West reset effects. The weekly aggregation also means that short-term spikes in pressure, for example following a single large influx of respiratory admissions, are partially obscured in the regional totals. Post-weekend and post-public holiday surges in trolley counts are a documented feature of the Irish hospital system, driven by reduced discharge rates over weekends and bank holidays [@department_of_health_macneill_2025]. While the New Year effect is explicitly modelled, other public holidays such as Easter and bank holidays generate comparable patterns of pre-holiday discharge and post-holiday surge that are not separately accounted for in the current framework.

The model treats each HSE region as independent, with no mechanism for shared temporal variation across regions. The residual cross-correlations observed across all 15 region pairs suggest this assumption is violated in practice. Extensions incorporating a multivariate structure, shared latent component, or spatial random effect could better account for system-wide shocks that affect all regions simultaneously, such as national respiratory illness surges or policy changes, and would be a natural direction for future work.

The AR(2) persistence parameter $\phi$ is shared across all six regions, which may not fully capture heterogeneity in the speed at which congestion dissipates across different regional health systems. It is plausible that regions with greater bed capacity or more developed discharge infrastructure recover more quickly from elevated pressure, a question that a region-specific AR(2) extension could address. Such an extension would however require careful consideration of identifiability given the length of the available series.

The Mid West reset term was motivated by a known system-wide overcrowding response implemented in the region in August 2024, and its inclusion was therefore justified on operational grounds prior to model fitting. However, the model does not systematically test whether comparable structural breaks occurred in other regions during the observation period, nor does it account for other unobserved system-level interventions that may have affected trolley rates. Extending the framework to detect and model such events across all regions, rather than specifying them a priori, represents a useful direction for future work.

The observation period of approximately three years, while sufficient to identify the main seasonal and calendar features of the data, limits the ability to assess longer-run trends in hospital pressure or to evaluate the stability of the regional ranking over multiple years. As the HSE health region structure was only established in 2023, extending the analysis to future data will be important for confirming whether the west/east divide identified here reflects a stable structural feature of the system or is partly an artefact of the transition period.

A further limitation is that the analysis does not account for structural covariates that may explain some of the observed regional variation, such as differences in bed capacity, staffing levels, population age structure, external facilities that can alleviate pressure or socioeconomic deprivation. Incorporating such covariates within a Bayesian regression framework would allow the analysis to distinguish between variation in trolley pressure attributable to measurable system characteristics and variation that reflects more fundamental differences in regional demand relative to capacity.

The estimated peaks cluster in the weeks around the New Year (Appendix Table \@ref(tab:results-phase-table)). A shared phase with region-specific amplitude could lead to a more parsimonious model, testing whether regions differ in the strength of the winter peak rather than its timing.

The annual cycle is currently a calendar term with no external driver. Winter trolley peaks coincide with respiratory illness admissions. Supplementing the cycle with a respiratory surveillance covariate, such as weekly influenza or RSV positivity from national surveillance, would move the model from describing the winter peak to explaining it. It would also let the peak respond to severity that varies year to year.

The model is fit to describe and rank the regions, not to predict. The same structure of regional level, annual cycle, and autoregressive terms can produce short-term forecasts of trolley counts. Forecasts would need out-of-sample evaluation with proper scoring rules, rather than the in-sample DIC used here. A validated forecast would give the HSE advance warning of regional surges.

The trolley counts come from a single source, the HSE daily report. The Irish Nurses and Midwives Organisation publishes an independently collected daily trolley count. Comparing the two series would test the reliability of the reported figures and flag hospitals where the counts diverge, such as the under-reporting observed at one Model 4 hospital.

# Conclusion \label{chap:conclusion}

This study develops a Bayesian time series framework for analysing weekly hospital trolley rates across the six HSE health regions of Ireland. Starting from a regional level with an autoregressive component, the model is extended iteratively to include an annual cycle, region-specific New Year block effects, and a Mid West operational reset term for August to September 2024. The selected specification uses an AR(2) persistence term; three sub-annual cycle extensions were tested but did not improve DIC. The model captures the dominant temporal features of the data within a parsimonious framework.

The central finding is a pronounced and statistically credible west/east divide in hospital pressure levels, with HSE Mid West carrying the highest burden and HSE Dublin and North East the lowest. This ordering is robust across all four scaling denominators examined (Table \@ref(tab:results-rank-table)) and is supported by near-certain pairwise posterior comparisons across fourteen of the fifteen region pairs. These findings are entirely invisible in raw regional totals and emerge only through population standardisation, reinforcing the importance of per capita scaling when making regional comparisons within a nationally administered health system. Five of six regions exhibit a winter-peaking annual cycle consistent with respiratory illness demand, and both the New Year dip and the Mid West operational reset are clearly identified as distinct features of the data.

By embedding these dynamics within a Bayesian framework, the analysis propagates uncertainty through to all model parameters and provides a principled basis for probabilistic regional comparison. Future work could explore daily-resolution modelling, region-specific AR parameters, and the incorporation of structural covariates such as bed capacity, staffing, and population age structure to help explain the observed regional disparities. As the HSE six-region structure was only established in 2023, extending the analysis as additional data accumulate will be important for confirming whether the west/east divide is a stable structural feature of the Irish hospital system. More broadly, this study demonstrates that Bayesian time series modelling of routinely collected administrative data can yield substantive, policy relevant insights into the distribution of hospital pressure across a national health system, supporting more evidence-informed approaches to capacity planning and resource allocation. An interactive version of these results is available at hsereport.ie.

\newpage

# References

<div id="refs"></div>

\newpage

# Appendix

## Annual Cycle Peak Phase

```{r results-phase-table}
# Phase stats are computed by thesis/phase_table.py -> phase_table.csv
phase_tab <- read_csv("phase_table.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  transmute(
    Region                    = region,
    `Mean peak (wk from NY)`  = mean_pk,
    `95% CI (wk from NY)`     = sprintf("[%+.1f, %+.1f]", ci_lo, ci_hi),
    `P(winter)`               = p_winter
  )

knitr::kable(phase_tab, digits = c(0, 1, 0, 3),
             booktabs = TRUE, linesep = "", caption = "Peak week of the annual cycle relative to New Year") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```


## Model Development DIC {#app:dev-dic}

The model is built up one component at a time. In general, each is retained when it lowers DIC.

```{r app-dev-dic}
dev <- "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k"
dev_labels <- c(
  v4.1 = "Regional level",
  v4.2 = "+ Annual cycle",
  v4.3 = "+ Regional New Year",
  v4.4 = "+ Mid West reset",
  v4.5 = "+ AR(1)",
  v4.6 = "+ AR(2)"
)
dev_tab <- bind_rows(lapply(names(dev_labels), function(v) {
  d <- read_csv(file.path(dev, v, "dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
  tibble(Description = dev_labels[[v]], Deviance = d$deviance, pD = d$penalty, DIC = d$DIC)
}))
knitr::kable(dev_tab, digits = 1, booktabs = TRUE, linesep = "",
             caption = "Model development sequence: DIC as each component is added (per 10,000 population).") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```


## Results of Regional Structure Changes {#app:regional-structure}

Each ablation is the reported model with one feature collapsed to a single value shared across all regions. This isolates the value of the regional level and the regional annual cycle inside the reported model.

```{r app-regional-structure-dic}
rs <- "../data/models/wide_weekly_scaledPer10k"
rs_ref <- read_csv(file.path(rs, "v4.6", "dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)$DIC
rs_rows <- list(
  c("v4.6",  "Reported model (regional level, regional cycle)"),
  c("v4.12", "Global level (level shared across regions)"),
  c("v4.13", "Shared annual cycle (cycle shared across regions)")
)
rs_tab <- bind_rows(lapply(rs_rows, function(r) {
  d <- read_csv(file.path(rs, r[1], "dic.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
  tibble(
    Model        = r[2],
    Deviance     = d$deviance,
    pD           = d$penalty,
    DIC          = d$DIC,
    `DIC change` = ifelse(r[1] == "v4.6", "", sprintf("%+.1f", d$DIC - rs_ref))
  )
}))
knitr::kable(rs_tab, digits = 1, booktabs = TRUE, linesep = "",
             caption = "Regional-structure ablations of the reported model. Each row is the reported model with one feature collapsed to a single value shared across regions. DIC change is the ablation minus the reported model. A positive change means the regional feature lowers DIC and is justified. A negative change means a shared feature fits as well or better.") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```


## Pairwise Regional Level Comparisons {#app:alpha-pairwise}

Each row is the posterior difference between two regional levels ($\alpha_j$), per 10,000 population. An interval that excludes 0 means the two regions differ at the 95% level.

```{r results-alpha-pairwise}
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
regions <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands", "HSE Dublin and North East",
  "HSE Dublin and South East", "HSE Mid West",
  "HSE South West", "HSE West and North West"
)
alpha_mat <- sapply(seq_along(regions),
                    function(i) raw[[paste0("alpha[", i, "]")]])
colnames(alpha_mat) <- regions

pairs <- combn(regions, 2, simplify = FALSE)
pair_rows <- lapply(pairs, function(p) {
  d <- alpha_mat[, p[1]] - alpha_mat[, p[2]]
  if (mean(d) < 0) { d <- -d; p <- rev(p) }
  q <- quantile(d, c(0.025, 0.975))
  tibble(
    Comparison  = p[1],
    vs          = p[2],
    `Mean diff` = mean(d),
    `95% CI`    = sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]", q[1], q[2]),
    `Contains 0`= ifelse(q[1] <= 0 & q[2] >= 0, "Yes", "No")
  )
})
pair_tab <- bind_rows(pair_rows) |> arrange(desc(`Mean diff`))

knitr::kable(pair_tab, digits = 2, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, linesep = "", caption = "Pairwise comparison of regional levels (\\(\\alpha_j\\)) per 10,000 population.") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "repeat_header")
```


## Pairwise Cycle Amplitude Comparisons {#app:amplitude-pairwise}

Each row is the posterior difference between two regional annual cycle amplitudes ($A_j$), per 10,000 population. Every interval contains 0, so no two regions are credibly separated on amplitude.

```{r results-amplitude-pairwise}
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
regions <- c(
  "HSE Dublin and Midlands", "HSE Dublin and North East",
  "HSE Dublin and South East", "HSE Mid West",
  "HSE South West", "HSE West and North West"
)
A_mat <- sapply(seq_along(regions), function(i) {
  sqrt(raw[[paste0("beta[", i, "]")]]^2 +
       raw[[paste0("gamma[", i, "]")]]^2)
})
colnames(A_mat) <- regions

pairs <- combn(regions, 2, simplify = FALSE)
amp_pair <- bind_rows(lapply(pairs, function(p) {
  d <- A_mat[, p[1]] - A_mat[, p[2]]
  if (mean(d) < 0) { d <- -d; p <- rev(p) }
  q <- quantile(d, c(0.025, 0.975))
  tibble(
    Comparison  = p[1],
    vs          = p[2],
    `Mean diff` = mean(d),
    `95% CI`    = sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]", q[1], q[2]),
    `Contains 0`= ifelse(q[1] <= 0 & q[2] >= 0, "Yes", "No")
  )
})) |> arrange(desc(`Mean diff`))

knitr::kable(amp_pair, digits = 2, booktabs = TRUE, longtable = TRUE, linesep = "", caption = "Annual cycle amplitude pairwise") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = "repeat_header")
```


## Raw Weekly Counts by Region

```{r app-raw-boxplot, fig.cap="Distribution of weekly on trolley counts by HSE region across the full series (Jan 2023 -- Apr 2026). Box shows the IQR, central line the median; points beyond are individual weeks.", fig.width=8, fig.height=3}
ggplot(weekly, aes(x = reorder(region, total_trolleys, FUN = median, decreasing = TRUE),
                   y = total_trolleys, fill = region)) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.6, alpha = 0.85, outlier.size = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = region_palette, guide = "none") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Weekly on trolley counts") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =11) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = 1))
```


## Residual Normality Checks {#app:residuals}

```{r app-resid-qq, fig.cap="Normal quantile-quantile plots of the standardised residuals by region. Points on the red line indicate normality.", fig.width=9, fig.height=5, fig.pos="H"}
res <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "residuals_posterior_mean.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
regions <- names(res)
tau_mean <- sapply(seq_along(regions),
                   function(i) mean(raw[[paste0("tau[", i, "]")]]))

resid_long <- res |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "region", values_to = "r") |>
  mutate(tau = tau_mean[match(region, regions)],
         std = r * sqrt(tau))

ggplot(resid_long, aes(sample = std, colour = region)) +
  stat_qq(size = 0.7) +
  stat_qq_line(colour = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ region, ncol = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_palette, guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "Theoretical quantile (N(0,1))",
       y = "Standardised innovation residual") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =10)
```

```{r app-resid-hist, fig.cap="Histograms of the standardised residuals by region with the standard normal density overlaid.", fig.width=9, fig.height=5, fig.pos="H"}
res <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "residuals_posterior_mean.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
regions <- names(res)
tau_mean <- sapply(seq_along(regions),
                   function(i) mean(raw[[paste0("tau[", i, "]")]]))

resid_long <- res |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "region", values_to = "r") |>
  mutate(tau = tau_mean[match(region, regions)],
         std = r * sqrt(tau))

ggplot(resid_long, aes(std, fill = region)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 30, colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ region, ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = region_palette, guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "Standardised residual", y = "Density") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =10)
```


## Residual Cycles

```{r app-resid-psd, fig.cap="Power spectral density of the posterior-mean innovation residuals by region (period in weeks, log scale). Peaks mark periodic structure remaining after the annual cycle, New Year, and Mid West reset terms and the AR(2) correction.", fig.width=8, fig.height=4, fig.pos="H"}
res <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "residuals_posterior_mean.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

psd_one <- function(x, label) {
  s <- spectrum(x, plot = FALSE, detrend = TRUE)
  tibble(period = 1 / s$freq, power = s$spec, region = label)
}
psd_df <- bind_rows(lapply(names(res),
                           function(r) psd_one(res[[r]], r)))

ggplot(psd_df, aes(period, power, colour = region)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.5) +
  scale_x_log10(breaks = c(2, 3, 4, 6, 10, 13, 17, 22, 30, 40, 52)) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_palette) +
  labs(x = "Period (weeks, log scale)", y = "Power", colour = NULL) +
  theme_thesis(base_size =10) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r app-resid-pacf, fig.cap="Partial autocorrelation of the posterior-mean innovation residuals by region, to lag 52 weeks. Dashed bands are the 95% white-noise bounds. Bars beyond them mark week-to-week dependence not captured by the AR(2) term.", fig.width=9, fig.height=5, fig.pos="H"}
res <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "residuals_posterior_mean.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

pacf_one <- function(x, label) {
  p <- pacf(x, plot = FALSE, lag.max = 52)
  tibble(lag = as.numeric(p$lag), pacf = as.numeric(p$acf),
         region = label)
}
pacf_df <- bind_rows(lapply(names(res),
                            function(r) pacf_one(res[[r]], r)))
ci <- qnorm(0.975) / sqrt(nrow(res))

ggplot(pacf_df, aes(lag, pacf, colour = region)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-ci, ci),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = lag, yend = 0)) +
  facet_wrap(~ region, ncol = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_palette, guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "Lag (weeks)", y = "PACF") +
  theme_thesis(base_size =10)
```

## Trace Plots and Convergence {#app:traces}

Convergence diagnostics for the selected model. Trace plots show all monitored parameters across the four chains after burn-in. The Gelman-Rubin table reports the potential scale reduction factor (R-hat) for every parameter.

Throughout this section the bracketed index on a parameter denotes the HSE region: 1 Dublin and Midlands, 2 Dublin and North East, 3 Dublin and South East, 4 Mid West, 5 South West, 6 West and North West. The four New Year window effects are numbered in temporal order: delta_1 the week two weeks before the New Year week, delta_2 the week before, delta_3 the New Year week (containing Jan 1), and delta_4 the week after.

```{r app-trace-setup, include=FALSE}
raw <- read_csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "raw_samples.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# Relabel the four New Year window effects in temporal order (display only).
relabel_delta <- function(x) {
  x <- sub("^delta_pm1",  "delta_1", x)
  x <- sub("^delta_pre",  "delta_2", x)
  x <- sub("^delta_mid",  "delta_3", x)
  x <- sub("^delta_post", "delta_4", x)
  x
}
names(raw) <- relabel_delta(names(raw))

params <- sort(setdiff(names(raw), "chain"))
family <- sub("\\[.*$", "", params)
family <- sub("^(sigma_w)[0-9]+$", "\\1", family)
family <- sub("^(phi)[0-9]+$", "\\1", family)
groups <- split(params, factor(family, levels = unique(family)))

long <- raw |>
  group_by(chain) |>
  mutate(iter = row_number()) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(chain = factor(chain)) |>
  pivot_longer(all_of(params), names_to = "param", values_to = "value")

trace_panel <- function(p) {
  d <- dplyr::filter(long, param == p)
  tr <- ggplot(d, aes(iter, value, colour = chain)) +
    geom_line(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.15) +
    geom_smooth(se = FALSE, method = "loess", span = 0.2, linewidth = 0.4) +
    labs(title = p, x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_thesis(base_size =7) +
    theme(legend.position = "none",
          plot.title = element_text(size = 7),
          axis.text.x  = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks.x = element_blank())
  kd <- ggplot(d, aes(value, colour = chain)) +
    geom_density(linewidth = 0.4) +
    coord_flip() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_thesis(base_size =7) +
    theme(legend.position = "none",
          axis.text  = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks = element_blank(),
          panel.grid = element_blank())
  tr + kd + plot_layout(widths = c(3, 1))
}

trace_cap <- "Trace with LOWESS fit (left) and posterior density (right) for every monitored parameter of the selected model; panel titles give the parameter. The horizontal axis is the iteration over the 20{,}000 retained draws, shared across all panels. Four chains overlaid after burn-in, all retained draws shown. The bracketed index on a parameter denotes the HSE region: 1 is Dublin and Midlands, 2 is Dublin and North East, 3 is Dublin and South East, 4 is Mid West, 5 is South West, 6 is West and North West."
```

```{r app-trace, fig.width=9, fig.height=9, out.width="100%", fig.cap=trace_cap, fig.pos="H"}
all_panels <- lapply(unlist(groups, use.names = FALSE), trace_panel)
patchwork::wrap_plots(all_panels, ncol = 5)
```


```{r app-gelman, results='asis'}
gelman <- read.csv(
  file.path(selected_model, "gelman.csv"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE,
  check.names = FALSE
)
gelman$param <- relabel_delta(gelman$param)
gelman <- gelman[order(gelman$param), c("param", "Point est.")]
names(gelman) <- c("Parameter", "R-hat")

# split into two halves laid side by side to save vertical space
n    <- nrow(gelman)
half <- ceiling(n / 2)
left  <- gelman[1:half, ]
right <- gelman[(half + 1):n, ]
if (nrow(right) < nrow(left)) {
  right <- rbind(right, data.frame(Parameter = "", `R-hat` = NA, check.names = FALSE))
}
two_col <- cbind(left, right)

options(knitr.kable.NA = "")
kableExtra::kbl(
  two_col,
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  digits    = 4,
  row.names = FALSE,
  linesep   = "",
  col.names = c("Parameter", "R-hat", "Parameter", "R-hat"),
  caption   = "Gelman-Rubin potential scale reduction factor (R-hat) for every monitored parameter of the selected model. Values at or near 1.00 indicate convergence; the maximum observed was 1.0002."
) |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"))
```
